entry.title: How to post an Accounting Document with multiple currency codes
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I've a requirement to post an Accounting Document via BAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST with multiple currency codes line items.

Ex: Below line items should be posted as a single document.

Transaction CurrencyAmount in TCLocal CurrencyAmount in LC
USD17.25KRW25000
AED63.36KRW25000
SGD46.87-KRW50000-

But I'm getting error that USD 17.25 is not balanced, however the local currency amount is balanced.

Expecting a suitable solution to achieve the same.

Regards,

Raja

entry.title: Re: Adobe form - Formcalc/Javascript How can we get the l...
entry.content:
For the benefit of all here is the solution applied.

1. We need a flowed empty subform between the "frm_Signature" and the previous subform

  • subform "frm_Detail"
  • subform "frm_Filler"
  • subform "frm_Signature"

2.  At the event "ready:layout" of the "frm_Signature" subform add the following Javascript

var subform = "frm_Signature" ;
var position = 190 ; // Desire bottom position in mm 
var page = xfa.layout.absPageCount() - 1 ;
var objs = xfa.layout.pageContent(page, "subform") ;
for (var i=0; i<objs.length; i++) {
   var som = objs.item(i).somExpression ;
   if ( som.indexOf(subform) != -1 ){
      var object = objs.item(i);
      break;
   }
}
var objectH = xfa.layout.h( object, "mm" );   
var objectY = xfa.layout.y( object, "mm" );
if ( ( objectY + objectH ) < position ) {
   this.parent.frm_Filler.h = position - ( objectY + objectH ) + "mm";  // Assign height of frm_Filler
   xfa.layout.relayout();
}

This will assign the height of the "frm_Filler" subform and relaunch the layout process.

entry.content:
Hello all,

I've worked a long time using BO - Web Intelligence.

Here I was able to define references for any cell in a table. This references were then available globally. This meant that a row could be created in another table in which the referenced cell from the first table was available for calculations.

Now I have similar requirements that I need to implement in SAC. Unfortunately, this functionality doesn't seem to exist here. I miss the flexibility that I know from various BO tools. Does anyone has an idea how this could be implemented? Is there perhaps a reference method that I haven't discovered yet?

Thanx!

entry.title: HCM Brasil: Como deve ser informada a rubrica de plano de saúde no eSocial S-1.3?
entry.content:
Desde que o governo alterou a Tabela 21 - Códigos de Incidência Tributária da Rubrica para o IRRF do eSocial, incluindo o código “67 - Plano privado coletivo de assistência à saúde” no grupo Dedução do rendimento tributável do IRRF, surgiram diversas interpretações sobre como reportar essa rubrica no evento S-1010 – Tabela de Rubricas.

O entendimento da SAP sempre foi de que a inclusão desse código no leiaute representava um erro, uma vez que os descontos de plano de saúde não podem ser abatidos da base de cálculo do rendimento tributável para fins de retenção mensal do Imposto de Renda.

No dia 18 de dezembro de 2024, foi publicada uma nova informação (item 14.5) na página de FAQ do eSocial ratificando a interpretação da SAP.  

Veja aqui um trecho dessa nova informação disponível:

“...No entanto, o valor descontado pela empresa a título de custeio do plano de saúde não pode ser abatido da base de cálculo do rendimento tributável para fins de retenção do Imposto de Renda na fonte. Assim, esse desconto apenas transita na folha de pagamento do trabalhador, sem impactar a base de cálculo do IRRF. Nesse contexto, o uso de Código de Incidência de IRRF com valor "9" ou "67" não altera o valor final da retenção....”

Até então, a solução da SAP gerava as rubricas relacionadas à plano de saúde com código de incidência de IRRF da rubrica como “79 - Outras isenções”.

Embora essa configuração não impactasse o valor final da retenção do IRRF mensal, ajustamos nossa solução para que as rubricas de desconto de plano de saúde sejam geradas com o código de incidência de IRRF como “09 - Verba transitada pela folha de pagamento de natureza diversa de rendimento ou retenção/isenção/dedução de IR”. A SAP Note 3552889 - eSocial - S-1010 - codIncIRRF for medical assistance wage-types já está disponível para implementar essa adequação.

Além disso, a solução também permite a geração das rubricas de desconto de plano de saúde com código de incidência de IRRF da rubrica igual a “67 - Plano privado coletivo de assistência à saúde”, caso o cliente opte por essa configuração. Para tanto, é necessário seguir as instruções da SAP Note 3532823 - eSocial - S-1010 - New health plan option to report IRRF code.

Em resumo:

  • Anteriormente: As rubricas de desconto de plano de saúde eram geradas com o código de incidência de IRRF igual a “79”.
  • Agora: A partir da SAP Note 3552889 - eSocial - S-1010 - codIncIRRF for medical assistance wage-types, o sistema passa a gerar as rubricas de desconto de plano de saúde com código de incidência de IRRF da rubrica igual a “9”.
  • Opção adicional: Para gerar as rubricas de desconto de plano de código de incidência de IRRF da rubrica igual a “67”, o cliente deve configurar a rubrica conforme a SAP Note 3532823 - eSocial - S-1010 - New health plan option to report IRRF code.

Gostou desse post? Dê um Like e compartilhe o conteúdo com seus colegas.

Fiquem à vontade para deixar um feedback, comentário ou pergunta sobre este Blog post no espaço abaixo. E não esqueçam de seguir a tag HCM Payroll Brazil na SAP Community para ficarem ligados nas últimas notícias.

Um abraço,        

Leandro D’Avila

entry.title: Down Payment Request for Italy allows Gross Price only
entry.content:
Dear SAP support,

please open Workcenter "Sales Orders" --> "Sales Orders" and search for sales order ***.

Please carry out the following steps for the sales order:

1. Marking the sales order line and clicking on "Create Down payment"
2. Entering "External Reference = Test", "Amount = 10EUR", "Tax Country/Region = Italy"

When entering "Italy" as country, the checkbox for "Gross / Net indicator" is automatically set. We've observed this behaviour only for Italy. When entering a different county, the checkbox isn't set.

We don't want to invoice gross and therefore do not want the checkbox to be ticked. Unfortunately, we cannot uncheck the box manually. Please let us know where this can be changed and where it's configured that the system behaves like this for Italy.

You can also take a look at sales order ***. In the follow-up process we have problems due to the tax code *** and receive inconsistent invoice requests (see e.g. invoice request **). The checkbox shouldn't be checked here either.

As the down payments in the production system must be created urgently with tax code ***, which is not possible at the moment, we need a solution as soon as possible.

Best regards


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Down Payment Request for Italy allows Gross Price only
entry.content:
Dear customer,

As per our analysis, this is the expected system behavior.

Due to legal requirements, Business ByDesign's system only allows you to release Down Payment Requests in Gross Price for Italy.

This behavior occurs since the System Release 1902 and is documented on KBA 2760906 - Down Payment Requests only with Gross Price [Italy Only] (https://me.sap.com/notes/2760906).

Best Regards,

Marcelo
SAP Product Support

entry.title: Global User ID in SAP Build Work Zone
entry.content:
The Global User ID (userUUID) plays a important role in integration scenarios, acting as a consistent user identifier across system landscapes. This is especially important for scenarios like API-based notification services, where a common identifier ensures seamless data flow and user correlation.

There are two approaches for implementing the Global User ID, but we recommend the first of the two:

Approach 1:

Use SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Authentication and Identity Provisioning to generate and distribute Global User ID. In this case, the attribute is automatically generated by Identity Authentication at user creation. Its value is populated in the Global User ID field for every newly created, imported or provisioned user. After that, Identity Provisioning distributes it to various SAP applications.

Approach 2:

Use your own value for Global User ID and distribute it to SAP applications with SAP Cloud Identity Services. In this case the Identity Authentication is used as a user store, it receives the value in the Global User ID field for every newly created, imported or provisioned user. After that, Identity Provisioning distributes it to various SAP applications. Because the Global User ID field cannot be empty, if you do not provide a value for Global User ID, Identity Authentication generates one for your users. When distributing your own value without SAP Cloud Identity Services, you use your existing identity management processes to provision it to SAP applications (see  the dotted line in the diagram below).

 

Prerequisites and usage

  • Users must have Identity Authentication and Identity Provisioning configured in their landscape.
  • The Global User ID identifier is immutable and unique across technology layers such as user interfaces, APIs, and security tokens, as well as across products and lines of business.
  • It is recommended to use the Global User ID of SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Authentication as the federation identifier.
  • Identity Authentication hosts the Global User ID while Identity Provisioning provisions the Global User ID to other systems in the user’s landscape.

 

How to leverage Global ID in SAP Build Work Zone

Provisioning the Global User ID

First, the Global ID is provisioned to SAP Build Work Zone via SCIM API, and consequently transferred to SAP Build Work Zone, as to any other SAP solution, via Identity Provisioning. This applies to both:

  • Role-mapping for content providers within SAP Build Work Zone, all editions as an alternative to role collection-based mapping. See here for more details.
  • Digital Workplace Service (DWS) persistence layer specific to SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition and SAP SuccessFactors Work Zone. This process is part of the Work Zone onboarding. More details about this can be found here.

The Global User ID is transferred (in both scenarios) via the target system transformation / mapping in Identity Provisioning. Using the provided (default) transformation from the SAP Build Work Zone help guide will ensure the attribute is provisioned as required. You will need to verify that the Identity Provisioning source system (for example Cloud Identity) has the Global User ID available.

For example, SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition target system:

 

Trust Setup and Subject Name Identifier

It is recommended to use the Global User ID as the subject name identifier for the trust setup - for both the BTP Subaccount level and direct trust with SAP Build Work Zone. If you want to learn more about the direct OpenID Connect-based trust setup, please see the following blog.

Depending on the configuration of the ‘JAM’ destination, specifically the userIdSource property, it will also require the Global User ID in additional fields – specifically the SCIM.userName. To learn more about the authentication flow and corresponding field usage, please see here.

 

Verify Global User ID information in SAP Build Work Zone (advanced)

To verify the Global ID is properly created in the SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition / SAP SuccessFactors Work Zone, you can check the users using and directly modifying when needed, the SCIM API. This is the same API also utilized by Identity Provisioning (Work Zone target system). When using the API, you can find the Global User ID stored in a dedicated field in the SCIM user record:

For more details related to the SCIM API of advanced edition, how to connect to it, and more, please see the dedicated section in the help guide.

Finally, you can run a report in SAP Build Work Zone administration console by selecting the Company User Detail report (for both internal and external users). This will provide you with the list of all users provisioned to the system and the user values for SCIM.userName, SCIM.id (and others) which can be used as filters / parameters in the SCIM API. Note that in the report the User ID refers to the SCIM.userName and the Uuid refers to SCIM.id.

 

Use Cases

Global User ID plays an important role in SAP Build Work Zone, especially in integration scenarios. For instance, in SAP Task Center, the Global User ID functions as a correlation attribute, linking user identities across different systems. This ensures that tasks from multiple systems can be reliably associated with the same user, enabling a unified task management experience. The integration with the Central Notification Service, which allows Work Zone users to access notifications from various systems, also requires the Global User ID mapping.

Details on this integration setup can be found here (for Task Center) & here (for Notifications).

Another worth mentioning use case is Guided Experiences, where the Global User ID is needed to communicate between SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), SAP Build Process Automation (SBPA), and SAP Build Work Zone. This is instrumental for the UI Wizard in SAP Build Work Zone to retrieve and utilise processes created in SBPA, streamlining workflows and ensuring smooth integration across systems.

Lastly, when creating UI integration cards, the Global User ID is also provided / exposed as user context and can be referenced by the developer of the UI Cards. More details on this built-in card context is available in the SAP-samples GitHub repository:

 

For more info please check:

Help Guide: https://help.sap.com/docs/build-work-zone-advanced-edition/sap-build-work-zone-advanced-edition/run-configurator

Learning Journey & Certification: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/implement-and-administer-sap-build-work-zone/explaining-the-authentication-flow-of-sap-build-work-zone

entry.title: Re: Code List based on CDS View doesn't show View Entitie...
entry.content: There the following note is given: If data is requested, the method set_data must be called. If the total number of records is requested, the method set_total_number_of_records must be called. Otherwise there will be an error during runtime. The io_response->set_data( ) method is only allowed to fill elements listed by io_request->get_requested_elements( ). System may react with a failure or a short dump if these rules aren't followed. In UI scenarios with Fiori Elements the total number of records is always requested by the UI.

entry.title: Re: Dynamic Parameter Asking for Login Information
entry.content:
No, not by default. CR will never retain passwords, huge security breach if anyone can access the data source, they could modify the report and access data they should not be able to see....

Talk to your ERP Administrator, they may be able to set the password for you depending on how they have implemented the reports. It is possible to set the log in info so it doesn't prompt but that all depends on how they have implemented the report security.

You can set the log on info in the report to use Trusted Authentication which should use the initial log on info when the report is saved. Refreshing with a new value should then use the existing DB connection info.

PS - If you are using Business One ERP system add the Business One Tag.

entry.title: Service with Advanced Execution: Process Flow 4 – Service Quotation with Catalogue Price
entry.content:
Service with Advanced Execution is a new service business process in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. This is based on the integration of Service with Maintenance Management.

The concept of Execution Status in Service with Advanced Execution is introduced in the following blog:

Execution Status in Service with Advanced Execution

Within Service with Advanced Execution, there are various process flows depending on the business scenario. These process flows are distinguished based on two aspects:

  • How the process starts, and which is the first transaction.
  • What is the trigger for the creation of the maintenance order and eventually the start of planning.

Process Flow 4:

This blog showcases the process flow wherein the initial transaction is the service order quotation, and the maintenance order is not created in the service order quotation phase.

For example, let us assume that a car dealership offers maintenance and repair services for various car models. A customer requests a standard service, such as an engine oil change or brake replacement, and asks for a quotation. The Service Manager has agreed with the customer to perform fixed price billing. The dealership maintains a price master that lists predefined prices for services and parts.

In such scenarios, the service manager will create a quotation to provide a cost estimate for the service requested by the customer. Since the cost of service is agreed upon with the help of existing price master, there is no need of a detailed planning through maintenance order.

Creation of Service Order Quotation

A service order quotation can be created using the ‘Create Service Quotation’ App.

The following details could be filled while creating a service order quotation document:

  • Sold-to-Party
  • Organizational data such as Sales Organization and optionally Service Organization
  • Parties involved such as Service Employee Group and Employee Responsible
  • Execution Order Item with Planning Plant, Work Center, DIP Profile and Billing Relevance
  • Reference Objects

After entering all the details, save the service order quotation document. The service order quotation gets created with an execution order item with lifecycle status as 'Open'.

Status lifecycle in Service with Advanced Execution

                                                           Current state of an execution order item

The state diagram illustrates the status lifecycle of one execution order item along with its corresponding maintenance order (if any) in this process flow. Each block below represents a state displaying 3 different status values. The lifecycle and execution status of an execution order item along with the status of the corresponding maintenance order.

 

Send to Customer

Once the cost estimate is derived for the requested service, either based on the price master or based on any manual intervention, the quotation can be sent to the customer for approval. When the service order quotation is sent to customer, the execution order item status is updated to 'Released'.

Accept and creation of Follow-up Service Order

When the customer accepts the service order quotation, its lifecycle is updated to 'Completed'. A follow-up service order is automatically created, carrying over the existing execution order item.

Further processing of the service order follows the regular process flow.

Summary

The distinct feature in this process flow is the cost estimation without a maintenance order in a service order quotation phase.

 

Further references

To know more about the execution order item and its attributes, please refer following blog:

Deep Dive: Execution Order Item and Billable Maintenance Order

entry.title: Receiving Electronic Invoices Through the Peppol Network in SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management
entry.content:
In this blog, we will explore how SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management can process electronic invoices received through the Peppol network.

As of January 2025, a new legal mandate in Germany requires businesses to be able to receive invoices electronically from their suppliers. For more information about the law in general, check out this blog New B2B electronic invoicing law in Germany: What you need to know.

Multiple Solutions Available

While this blog focuses on handling electronic invoices through the Peppol network, it's important to note that there are other solutions available. SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management also supports electronic invoices received by email. There are additional SAP offerings for handling electronic invoices received from your supplier. To get a complete list, check out the Supported Business Scenarios for Germany.

For more information on some of these alternatives, please refer to the following blogs: 

  • Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email in SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management
  • Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email in SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, or SAP ERP
  • Receiving Electronic Invoices Through the Peppol Network in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 
  • Receiving Electronic Invoices through the Peppol network in SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, or SAP ERP

Overview of the Process

The process of receiving  invoices through the Peppol network in SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management involves several key steps:

  1. Receiving the Invoice
    Your supplier sends an invoice through the Peppol network and the invoice is received at your receiver access point in SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition.
  2. Validating and Mapping the XML
    SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition validates the electronic invoice and maps it to the standard format used in SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management.
  3. Verifying and Posting Invoice
    If manual adjustments are needed, such as correcting missing or incorrect fields, you can edit the invoice using the Manage Supplier Invoices Centrally app    . Then you post the invoice.
  4. Further Processing
    An invoice for the received electronic document is created in your connected business system and you can further process it.

For more details about the process, see Receive Invoices from Peppol Network.

How to Get Started?

To establish the communication between the two services, you need to do some configurations as follows:

  • In SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management
    Create an instance of the SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management service with the inbound-drc service plan and generate a service key for this instance. See Service Keys.
  • In SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition
  1. Set up the connection from SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, Cloud Edition to SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management. This connection facilitates the Peppol Exchange process. See Setting Up Connectivity.
  2. Specify client credentials required for your system to communicate with SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition using OAuth 2.0 client authentication. See Creating Service Bindings Using OAuth 2.0 Client Authentication for Invoices Received Through the Peppol Network.

    Note: If you are interested in processing electronic invoices received by email, refer to Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email in SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management.

By following these steps, you can ensure compliance with Germany's new electronic invoicing law and streamline your invoicing processes using SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management.

entry.title: Cutover Business Activities Highlights for a SuccessFactors EC and ONB Implementation
entry.content:
This blog covers the implementation partners to go over the high level business cutover activities for XYZ Company for Employee Central and ONB2.0 , JPB Implementation with Refactoring of RCM, LMS.

Here I have taken the example of the implementation partner as ABC Inc.

Following picture depicts the high-level graphical cutover plan for the XYZ company.

In this picture the freeze is divided three- Master Data Freeze, Overall Freeze and Soft Freeze followed by technical go-live and go-live.

The duration is as follows. These durations can be shorter depending upon the project.

Master data freeze – 2 weeks

Overall Hard Freeze – 1 week

Soft Freeze – 1 week

Technical go live – 1 week

Go live – 1 week

Overview of the terminologies are explained as below ---

 

Master Data Freeze –

  • Only project team members can go and perform RCM. Provide access to all users who are assigned on open requisitions to perform RCM Activities.
  • Only project team members can go and perform ONB 1.0.
  • LMS Internal and External learners can continue learning.
  • ABC project team will do the Cutover as per the plan.
  • Critical / Non-Critical Activities related to Master data for XYZ to be recorded outside SuccessFactors.
  • Only critical transactions will happen in the  legacy systems. Non- critical transactions will be stopped in Legacy Systems as well.
  • Communication should be sent before the Cutover start period.

 

Overall Hard Freeze

  • XYZ  teams cannot get into the SF system
  • ABC project team will do the Cutover as per the plan.
  • Critical / Non-Critical Activities related to all modules for XYZ to be recorded outside SuccessFactors.
  • Only critical transactions will happen in the  legacy systems. Non- critical transactions will be stopped in Legacy Systems as well.
  • Communication should be sent before the Cutover start period.
  • No RCM and ONB1.0 Activity
  • Internal Learners will not have access to do learnings.

 

Soft Freeze

  • Only Project team members from XYZ can enter and do the Data catch up.
  • This will include all critical and non-critical transactions for Employee Master Data as given in Slide 10.
  • RCM Activities as per slide 10
  • LMS Assignment profile adjustments as per slide 10

Technical Go-Live

 

  • Technical Go live will provide HR users to enter data along with XYZ project Team members after technical go live 
  • HR Business can do all the transactions in SuccessFactors now.
  • ESS/MSS changes might need to be done through HR Business Team

 

Go –Live

  • ESS MSS Enabled and End users can now view and update their details.
  •  Legacy system no longer maintained

Now lets understand the XYZ GO LIVE Critical Items and how to handle them during Cutover-

Activities during Freeze period

Legacy System

SuccessFactors System

Hires

•        During Hard Freeze (April 14, 2025 – April 18, 2025) continue to hire until GOLIVE in Legacy system.

•        Employee id generation will happen in Legacy system.

•        During the soft freeze (April 21, 2025 – May 02, 2025)  data catchup will start in SF  by using Migration templates.

•        Reset Employee Id Sequence  in SF after Soft freeze

•        Keep gap for few employee  numbers  while resetting the Employee Id sequence.

Terminations

During Hard Freeze (April 14, 2025 – April 18, 2025) continue Terminations until GOLIVE in Legacy system

During the soft freeze (April 21, 2025 – May 02, 2025) data catchup will start in SF  by using Migration templates (if high in number) or Manual by inserting a term record and removing future conversion record.

Step progressions

During Hard Freeze (April 14, 2025 – April 18, 2025) continue  Step Progressions until GOLIVE in Legacy system.

During the soft freeze (April 21, 2025 – May 02, 2025) data catchup will start in SF. If the dates are after GO LIVE insert a record directly in SF else change the conversion record using Data Migration Templates

Pay changes

During Hard Freeze (April 14, 2025 – April 18, 2025) continue Pay changes  until GOLIVE in Legacy system

During the soft freeze (April 21, 2025 – May 02, 2025) data catchup will start in SF. If the dates are after GO LIVE insert a record directly in SF else change the conversion record using Data Migration Templates

Global Assignment

Concurrent Assignment

Contingent workers

During Hard Freeze (March 31, 2025 – April 18, 2025) continue  GA, CA and CW until GOLIVE in Legacy system

During the soft freeze (April 21, 2025 – May 02, 2025) data catchup will start in SF. If the dates are after GO LIVE insert a record directly in SF else change the conversion record using Data Migration Templates

Position creation

During Hard Freeze (March 31, 2025 – April 18, 2025) continue Position creation in Legacy during Hard Freeze.

During the soft freeze (April 21, 2025 – May 02, 2025) data catchup will start in SF. If the dates are after GO LIVE insert a record directly in SF else change the conversion record using Data Migration Templates.

Job requisition's

Completely stop during hard freeze (March 31, 2025 – April 18, 2025)

During Soft freeze (April 21, 2025 – May 02, 2025) close all requisitions from old template and forward applicants to new template.

Trainings

Complete stop during Hard Freeze (March 31, 2025 – April 18, 2025)

During Soft Freeze April 21, 2025 – May 02, 2025) Assignment profiles should be adjusted. Trainings will continue after Go-live

Now lets see how to handle XYZ GOLIVE NON Critical Transactions during cutover.

Non- Critical Activities during freeze period

Legacy System

SuccessFactors System

Non - Critical Transactions

•        Non-Critical Transactions will be stopped in Legacy systems during Hard Freeze.

•        During Soft Freeze noncritical  transactions  will be performed in Legacy system first before moving to SF.

•        All Non-Critical transactions should be completed before the Hard Freeze and communication should be sent to complete these transactions before hard freeze.

•        During Soft Freeze non-critical  transactions  will be imported via migration templates

•        Any transactions prior to FTSD date and freeze period should be performed in legacy system and then bring to SF Using data migration templates.

•        Any Exceptions of Non-Critical Job and Compensation transactions changes before the FTSD date might require a reload of Data conversion record on Effective dates entities such as Job and comp information OR Purge the employee and reloading him

entry.title: How to generate keys and decrypt audit reports from Ariba Intelligent Configuration Manager
entry.content:
More than once you need download the Ariba audit logs to make more analysis but you find that is not going to be easy because there is a key requested.

You find the help sap site explaning about it but it's no easy make all the described steps, here I will explain with details and step by step this process.

The steps to obtain the audit reports are the following:

  1. Download and install the Git program, only we need the Git Bash app but I recommend you install all by default (SAP recommends use Cygwin but Git works also). This program is neccesary to create the keys and decrypt the logs we need to export.

     

  2. Open the Git Bash program to create your private key and input the following command: openssl genrsa -out myPrivateKey.pem 2048.

     

  3.  Now you need to create your public key typing this command: 
    openssl rsa -in myPrivateKey.pem -pubout -out myPublicKey.pem.
  4. Open a file explorer window and find the folder in the path C:\Users\<<name of your user>> to check the 2 pem files were created.

    or type ls -l *.pem in the Git Bash console to check the existance of the files 

     

  5.  Login to Ariba and access to Intelligent Configuration Manager to generate the audit report. Depending the solution you have the access is diferent this site explains how to access and generate in detail.
  6. Using your favorite text editor, open the myPublicKey.pem file and copy all the content.
  7. Paste the content in the Public key box, make sure there are no empty spaces at the beginning and end of the Pulbic key box.

     

  8. Click in Generate report buttom, if all was made right the report is being generated until complete status and with download link in action colum, otherwise review the previous steps and generate new public and private keys if is necessary.

     

  9. Click in download link, a new window appears and click in download icon now the report zip encrypted is in your download folder.

     

  10. This zip file must have 3 files contained in it:
    • Key
    • IV
    • aes file
  11. Move this zip file to your user folder C:\Users\<<name of your user>> (were your pem files were created).
  12. Now you need decrypt the aes file using your private key file, IV file and Key file. For this you can use the code sample here from Help SAP site and continue to the step 16 or you can use my method that I will explain in the next step.
  13. Unzip the zip file in the same folder C:\Users\<<name of your user>> and don't delete the zip file.
  14. Using your favorite text editor paste this bash code:
    #!/bin/bash
    # Parameters
    PRIVATEKEY="myPrivateKey.pem"
    ZIPFILE="zip file you downloaded from Ariba Intelligent Configuration Manager"
    ENCSUFFIX=".aes"
    echo $ZIPFILE
    # Decrypt the Key and IV
    IV=$(openssl pkeyutl -decrypt -inkey myPrivateKey.pem -in IV)
    KEY=$(openssl pkeyutl -decrypt -inkey myPrivateKey.pem -in Key)
    # Look in the ZIP for files ending in .aes
    # This is the convention used to identify encrypted files
    FILELIST=$(unzip -Z1 "$ZIPFILE" | grep $ENCSUFFIX)
    #echo $FILELIST
    echo $KEY
    echo $IV
    # Decrypt the files that were found
    # Remove the .aes extension from the saved file
    for ENCFILE in $FILELIST
    do
    DECFILE=${ENCFILE%$ENCSUFFIX}
    echo $DECFILE
    echo $ENCFILE
    echo "Decrypting File: $ENCFILE --> $DECFILE"
    openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -d -K $KEY -iv $IV -in $FILELIST -out "$DECFILE"
    done
  15. Only modify the ZIPFILE parameter and add your zip file name like this.

     

  16. Save the file as decrypt.sh in your user folder C:\Users\<<name of your user>>, or use any name you want but make sure save it with .sh extension.
  17. Open again the Git Bash console and run the script: ./decrypt.sh 

     

  18. If everything was right you notice the existence of new zip file with the same name as aes file.
  19. Open or unzip this zip file and finally you will be able to open the excel audit report. 

Some considerations you may take are:

  • It's no neccesary create the pem files everytime you need generate the report.
  • Delete or overwrite the IV and Key file when you need decrypt the a new report.

And that's it, I hope it help you. 😉

This Article is complementary to SAP Help articles; Generating Audit Reports and Decrypting Audit Reports.

entry.title: Customer Onboarding for SAP Digital Manufacturing
entry.content:
Kickstart Your SAP Digital Manufacturing Journey with Confidence!

Starting a new project can feel overwhelming, but with the right guidance, you’ll hit the ground running. Our Customer Onboarding Journey is designed just for you—an SAP Digital Manufacturing newcomer—so you can move forward with clarity, purpose, and ease.

Where to Begin?

  1. Visit the SAP Digital Manufacturing Onboarding Resource Center

Think of it as your personal roadmap. Inside you will find:

  • How-To Guides: learn how to use SAP for Me step-by-step
  • Clear instructions: how to get started with your system
  • Project Insights: know what to expect and how to plan for your implementation
  1. Attend Our Onboarding Webcasts

Join our quarterly live sessions to make your onboarding journey effortless. You’ll gain access to expert tips, get your questions answered on the spot, and quickly feel confident using SAP Digital Manufacturing.

Can’t join us live? No worries—simply watch the on-demand recordings anytime you like.

Your Onboarding Journey: Four Phases to Success

Everything we share—from the videos and articles in the Onboarding Resource Center to our interactive Webcasts—is built around your onboarding timeline. At every step, you’ll know exactly what to do next and how to stay on track.

Ready to maximize the value of your SAP Digital Manufacturing project?

Explore these resources now and pave the way for lasting success:

 

This is the content in more detail:

Phase 1. Get Started with SAP 

Objective: Ensure you understand the SAP Digital Manufacturing provisioning process, get comfortable with important SAP tools and channel, enable smooth access to your system and how to request support when needed.

Key Highlights:

  • Get a clear picture of your onboarding journey
  • Explore SAP channels like SAP for Me and learn how to set up your s-user account
  • Access your system without hassle
  • Discover how to use SAP Support for both technical and non-technical questions

  

Phase 2. Plan your Implementation

Objective: Learn how to plan, run, and speed up your SAP Digital Manufacturing project using the tools included in your subscription.

Key Highlights:

  • Implement your project with the SAP Activate methodology
  • Keep track of your tasks efficiently with SAP Cloud ALM
  • Understand the importance of assigning clear roles and testing your system thoroughly

 

Phase 3. Get Enabled

 Objective: Get free access to learning resources and tools that help you fully understand and excel with your SAP Digital Manufacturing solution.

 Key Highlights:

  • Start with the SAP Digital Manufacturing Solution Overview
  • Find in-depth guides in the SAP Help Portal
  • Connect with fellow users and experts in the SAP Community
  • Boost your skills through SAP Enterprise Support learning opportunities

 

Phase 4. Prepare to Go Live

Objective: Make sure you have a smooth Go-Live experience and set the stage for long-term success.

Key Highlights:

  • Finish your final tasks confidently
  • Use SAP’s specialized support and services for Go-Live assistance
  • Access ongoing resources to keep improving after launch
  • Stay ahead by embracing new innovations once you’re live

 

In Conclusion

The SAP Digital Manufacturing onboarding journey is designed to guide you every step of the way—from signing your contract to a successful Go-Live. Make the most of each phase to learn, plan, and connect with all the resources available, ensuring long-term growth and achievement.

Your SAP Digital Manufacturing success story starts here—let’s shape the future together!

#FirstExperiencesLast
#SAPDigitalManufacturing

entry.title: Planning to Deploy SAP BW/4HANA 2023? Understand the Limitations and Possibilities for SAP BW 7.5.
entry.content:
Hi,

Greetings!

Are you planning for a Conversion to SAPBW/4HANA 2023, from SAP BW 7.5, please check out few notes to consider:

Pre-requisite for SAP BW7.50 version:

 

Incase already on SAP BW/4HANA 1.0, follow the simple and smooth 2 step Upgrade:

Step-1) SAP BW/4HANA 1.0 -> SAP BW/4HANA 2.0 -> SAP BW/4HANA 2023
Step-2) SAP BW/4HANA 1.0 -> SAP BW/4HANA 2021 -> SAP BW/4HANA 2023

Please note - Currently direct Upgrade from SAP BW 1.0 to SAP BW/4HANA 2023 is not possible!!!

Incase if you are planning any of the existing Conversion approaches based upon the existing SAP BW Version(SAP BW 7.x to BW4HANA 2021 or BW4HANA 2023), whether it is REMOTE or SHELL or In-Place, a few findings from my experience are listed for your reference before starting the actual Realization Phase of any Conversion:

1)Consider the Discovery Phase as knowledge platform, work closely with SAP-BASIS to implement Maintenance Planner.

2)Note down the findings of Readiness Check and Simplification List.

3)Align with the Upcoming /Planned Source-System Upgrades/Migrations.

4)Define the scope of Business Applications and carve out the unwanted flows.

5)Cleanup the Obsolete Source Systems along with the dependent Objects which are persistent in the system

6)Create a Production Copy of BW System and try to simulate the Conversion which gives more insights on the functionalities which are replaced with new Simplified Info Providers, Data Extraction Platform (ODP), Reporting Tools, Modelling Tools et.,

7)Perform data Consistency checks by leveraging the SAP Delivered Programs to handle the repairing of Master Data and SIDs, note down Listed Objects, this will help to analyze and get well-prepared for handling the actual data migration in Production Landscape.

8)Do the ABAP Code Compatibility Checks which can be initiated from Conversion Cockpit. This can be done multiple times to ensure the Sender System is ready for Conversion.

9)Go for periodic system Backups at various phases of Conversion.

10)List out the incompatible Extractors and enable the marker for ODP Extraction.

Consider the findings from Discovery Phase and carry forward the fixes, finding and alternative workarounds based upon the customization factor of data models and code, then will help to succeed the Transformation and plan the downtime of Production during Go-Live.

HAPPY Conversion! Get ready to enable Real-time Data Processing, Simplified modeling and Administration with prefabricated templates.

 

Many Thanks,

Sunil Babu Sanjamala

entry.title: How to upload Excel Spreadsheet on SAP Fiori UI5 Freestyle
entry.content:
Introduction

The Fiori UI5 demo kit works perfectly for most part of the examples. For this one particularly, my complain is that it does not import the file on the frontend, but the File Uploader calls the backend instead (or maybe I missed something)

From my perspective and my scenario, I needed something "simple" that could get all the spreadsheet data and call a service using JSON

Creating the popup to upload the file

To have a cleaner code, I decided to create the upload popup using  fragment

Create the button on your view to call the fragment

 

<Button text="Upload" press="onOpenUploadSheet"/>

 

Create the fragment like this one - please, remove what is not needed for your example.
Important to mention that if the property "sameFilenameAllowed" is false you will not be able to select the file, click on cancel, and click on the same file again (I thought it was a bug until find this property)

 

<c:FragmentDefinition xmlns="sap.m"
    xmlns:l="sap.ui.layout"
    xmlns:u="sap.ui.unified"
    xmlns:c="sap.ui.core"
    xmlns:up="sap.m.upload">
    <Dialog id="UploadSheetDialog" title="{i18n>UploadSheet}" busyIndicatorDelay="0">
        <u:FileUploader id="fileUploader" class="sapUiMediumMargin" tooltip="{18n>UploadSheetTooltip}" fileType="xlsx"
			placeholder="{i18n>UploadSheetPlaceholder}" typeMissmatch="onUploadSheeetTypeMismatch"
            change="onUploadSheetChange" sameFilenameAllowed="true" width="70%"/>
        <beginButton>
            <Button text="{i18n>upload}" press="onConfirmUploadSheet" type="Emphasized"/>
        </beginButton>
        <endButton>
            <Button text="{i18n>cancel}" press="onCancelUploadSheet" type="Reject"/>
        </endButton>
    </Dialog>
</c:FragmentDefinition>

 

Creating the Excel upload logic

On the view controller, create the following logic. The function onOpenUploadSheet is called by the button created on the view.xml.
If the user click on cancel, it will call onCancelUploadSheet

 

onOpenUploadSheet: function () {
      this._displayUploadSheet();
},

_displayUploadSheet: function () {
      this._countSheet = null;
      if (!this._oUploadSheetDialog) {
        Fragment.load({
          id: this.getView().getId(),
          name: "<your>.<project>.view.UploadSheet",
          controller: this,
        }).then(
          function (dialog) {
            this._oUploadSheetDialog = dialog;
            this.getView().addDependent(this._oUploadSheetDialog);
            this._oUploadSheetDialog.open();
          }.bind(this)
        );
      } else {
        this.byId("fileUploader").setValue(null).clear();
        this._oUploadSheetDialog.open();
      }
    },

onCancelUploadSheet: function () {
      this._countSheet = null;
      if (this._oUploadSheetDialog) {
        this._oUploadSheetDialog.close();
      }
},

 

A difference compared to https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=F1S0LOAkTak is that we cannot use getFocusDomRef() anymore (check this discussion https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-q-a/fileuploader-sapui5-version-1-103/qaq-p/12598852). To overcome this difference, we can use the event change, so I created the function "onUploadSheetChange".
I am simply storing the file uploaded into a global variable

 

onUploadSheetChange: function (oEvent) {
      this._countSheet = {
        file: oEvent.getParameter("files")[0],
        XLSXImport: true,
      };
},

 

When the user click on Confirm, it will call "onConfirmUploadSheet". This function checks if the global variable _countSheet is not null to proceed - please, import MessageBox if you want to use this control for error handling. The internal method _getText works to facilitate i18n texts

Once the file upload is completed, it will call the function "_onUploadSheetXSLXCompleted", which uses an external library to import the Excel spreadsheet

 

    onConfirmUploadSheet: function (oEvent) {
      if (!this._countSheet) {
        MessageBox.error(this._getText("noFileSelected"));
        return;
      }

      this.byId("UploadSheetDialog").setBusy(true);

      const reader = new FileReader();
      reader.onload = this._onUploadSheetXSLXCompleted.bind(this);
      reader.readAsBinaryString(this._countSheet.file);
    },

    _onUploadSheetXSLXCompleted: function (file) {
      try {
        const workbook = XLSX.read(file, {
          type: "binary",
        });

        const sheet = XLSX.utils.sheet_to_row_object_array(workbook.Sheets["Upload"]);
        
      } catch (error) {
        MessageBox.error(this._getText("xlsxCannotBeRead"));
        return [];
      }
    },

    _getText: function (sKey, args) {
      return this.getOwnerComponent().getModel("i18n").getResourceBundle().getText(sKey, args);
    },

 

The last step is to import the external library to Fiori. To do so, you can download the code from cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/xlsx/0.18.0/xlsx.js
Create a new folder called libs and create a file called xlsx.js with the code above

Open manifest.json and add a new resource on "sap.ui5"."resources" section 

 

"resources": {
      "js": [
        {
          "uri": "libs/xlsx.js"
        }
      ]
    },

entry.title: SAP ABAP: Explore Data Type Conversions with 30 Examples
entry.content:
Introduction

Data type conversions are fundamental in any programming language, and SAP ABAP is no exception. Whether you're converting strings to integers, handling dates, or managing packed numbers, understanding these conversions can enhance the robustness and accuracy of your ABAP applications. In this blog post, I'll show 30 examples of data type conversions in ABAP—some might seem obvious, but they often come with unexpected challenges and exceptions.

Data Type Conversions in ABAP

Below, I'll walk you through a series of data type conversions using a simple ABAP program. The program demonstrates different scenarios, including handling exceptions that arise during conversions.

1. Normal String

REPORT ztest_sk. 
DATA: lv_int     TYPE i,
      lv_date    TYPE d,
      lv_time    TYPE t,
      lv_numc(3) TYPE n,
      lv_str     TYPE string,
      lv_char    TYPE c LENGTH 3,
      lv_pack    TYPE p LENGTH 5 DECIMALS 2,
      lv_utc     TYPE utclong. 

lv_str = 'ABCD12.3'.
WRITE /: '01. String: '. WRITE lv_str.

2. String to Character

lv_char = lv_str.  
WRITE /: '02. String to Character3: '. WRITE lv_char.

Note: Data loss can occur when converting strings to smaller character fields, as shown in this example.

3. Exception: String to Character with EXACT #()

TRY.  
    lv_char = EXACT #( lv_str ).  
    WRITE /: lv_char.  
  CATCH cx_sy_conversion_data_loss.  
    WRITE /: '03. String to Character3 w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_DATA_LOSS exception'.  
ENDTRY.

Insight: Using EXACT #() with string to character conversion can result in exceptions if data loss is detected.

4. Alphanumeric String to Integer

TRY.  
    lv_int = lv_str.  
    WRITE /: lv_int.  
  CATCH cx_sy_conversion_no_number.  
    WRITE /: '04. String to integer: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_NUMBER exception'.  
ENDTRY.

Challenge: Converting non-numeric strings to integers triggers exception.

5. String to Date

lv_date = lv_str.  
WRITE /: '05. String to date: '. WRITE lv_date.

6. Exception: String to Date with EXACT #()

TRY.  
    lv_date = EXACT #( lv_str ).  
    WRITE /: lv_date.  
  CATCH cx_sy_conversion_no_date.  
    WRITE /: '06. String to date w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_DATE exception'.  
ENDTRY.

7. String to Time

lv_time = lv_str.  
WRITE /: '07. String to time: '. WRITE lv_time.

8. Exception: String to Time with EXACT #()

TRY.  
    lv_time = EXACT #( lv_str ).  
    WRITE /: lv_time.  
  CATCH cx_sy_conversion_no_time.  
    WRITE /: '08. String to time w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_TIME exception'.  
ENDTRY.

9. String to Number

lv_numc = lv_str.  
WRITE /: '09. String to number3: '. WRITE lv_numc.

10. Exception: String to Number with EXACT #()

TRY.  
    lv_numc = EXACT #( lv_str ).  
    WRITE /: lv_numc.  
  CATCH cx_sy_conversion_no_number.  
    WRITE /: '10. String to number3 w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_NUMBER exception'.  
ENDTRY.

11. Exception: String to Packed Number

TRY.  
    lv_pack = lv_str.  
    WRITE /: lv_pack.  
  CATCH cx_sy_conversion_no_number.  
    WRITE /: '11. String to Packed5_2: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_NUMBER exception'.  
ENDTRY.

12. Packed Number Handling

lv_pack =  '33.12456'.  
WRITE /: '12. Packed5_2 (33.12456): '. WRITE lv_pack.

13. Exception: Packed Number with EXACT #()

TRY.  
    lv_pack = EXACT #( 1 / 8 ).  
    WRITE /: lv_pack.  
  CATCH cx_sy_conversion_rounding.  
    WRITE /: '13. Packed5_2 (1/8) w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_ROUNDING exception'.  
ENDTRY.

14. Date Handling

DATA lv_date1 TYPE sy-datum.  
lv_date1 =  '20241233'.  
WRITE /: '14. Date (20241233): '. WRITE lv_date1 USING EDIT MASK '__/__/____'.

15. Exceptions: Date with EXACT #()

TRY.  
    lv_date = EXACT #( '202401233' ).  
    WRITE /: lv_date.  
  CATCH cx_sy_conversion_no_date.  
    WRITE /: '15. Date (20241233) w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_DATE exception'.  
ENDTRY.

16. Date to Integer

lv_int =  sy-datum.  
WRITE /: '16. Date to Integer (sy-datum) = Days from 01.01.0001): '.  
WRITE: sy-datum USING EDIT MASK '__/__/____', ' --> ', lv_int.

17. Integer to Date

lv_int = lv_int + 1.  
lv_date1 =  lv_int.  
WRITE /: '17. Integer to Date (sy-datum + 1) = Days from 01.01.0001): '.  
WRITE: lv_int, ' --> ', lv_date1 USING EDIT MASK '__/__/____'.

18. Time to Integer

lv_int =  sy-uzeit.  
WRITE /: '18. Time to Integer (sy-uzeit) = Seconds from 00:00:00): '.  
WRITE: sy-uzeit USING EDIT MASK '__:__:__', ' --> ', lv_int.

19. Integer to Time

DATA lv_time1 TYPE sy-uzeit.  
lv_int = lv_int + 3600.  
lv_time1 =  lv_int.  
WRITE /: '19. Integer to Time (sy-uzeit + 3600 sec) = Seconds from 00:00:00): '.  
WRITE: lv_int, ' --> ', lv_time1 USING EDIT MASK '__:__:__'.

20. Direct Date Difference

lv_date = '20240101'.  
lv_int = sy-datum - lv_date.  
WRITE /: '20. Date Difference (sy-datum) - ''20240101''): '. WRITE lv_int.

21. Character from Packed

lv_char = '1.23'.  
WRITE /: '21. Packed(1.23) to Character3: '. WRITE lv_char.

 

UTC Long Handling

Converting various data types to UTC long often results in exceptions since these formats might not be compatible.

22. Alphanumeric String to UTClong

TRY.
    lv_utc = lv_str.
    WRITE /: '22. String to UTC long: '. WRITE lv_utc.
  CATCH cx_sy_conversion_no_date_time.
    WRITE /: '22. String to UTC long: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_DATE_TIME exception'. WRITE lv_utc.
ENDTRY.

23. Character3 to UTClong

lv_char = lv_str.
TRY.
    lv_utc = lv_char.
    WRITE /: '23. Character3 to UTC long: '. WRITE lv_utc.
  CATCH cx_sy_conversion_no_date_time.
    WRITE /: '23. Character3 to UTC long: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_DATE_TIME exception'. WRITE lv_utc.
ENDTRY.

24. Numeric String to UTClong

TRY.
    lv_str = '20241220'.
    lv_utc = lv_str.
    WRITE /: '24. String (20241220) to UTC long: '. WRITE lv_utc.
  CATCH cx_sy_conversion_no_date_time.
    WRITE /: '24. String (20241220) to UTC long: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_DATE_TIME exception'. WRITE lv_utc.
ENDTRY.

25-29. Impossible UTC conversions

WRITE /: '25. Date can''t be converted to UTC long'.
WRITE /: '26. Time can''t be converted to UTC long'.
WRITE /: '27. Number can''t be converted to UTC long'.
WRITE /: '28. Integer can''t be converted to UTC long'.
WRITE /: '29. Packed can''t be converted to UTC long'.

30. System UTClong.

lv_utc = utclong_current( ).  
WRITE /: '30. System UTC time: '. WRITE lv_utc.

Conclusion

Navigating through data type conversions in ABAP can be a complex task that requires careful handling of exceptions and understanding of underlying data structures. By exploring these 30 examples, you gain a clearer perspective on how conversions impact data integrity and application logic.

Feel free to experiment with these examples in your own systems and unlock new insights into the world of ABAP programming.

Program Output:

01. String: ABCD12.3
02. String to Character3: ABC
03. String to Character3 w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_DATA_LOSS exception
04. String to integer: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_NUMBER exception
05. String to date: .312ABCD
06. String to date w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_DATE exception
07. String to time: ABCD12
08. String to time w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_TIME exception
09. String to number3: 123
10. String to number3 w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_NUMBER exception
11. String to Packed5_2: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_NUMBER exception
12. Packed5_2 (33.12456): 33,12
13. Packed5_2 (1/8) w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_ROUNDING exception
14. Date (20241233): 33/12/2024
15. Date (20241233) w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_DATE exception
16. Date to Integer (sy-datum) = Days from 01.01.0001): 20/12/2024 > 739.241
17. Integer to Date (sy-datum + 1) = Days from 01.01.0001): 739.242 > 21/12/2024
18. Time to Integer (sy-uzeit) = Seconds from 00:00:00): 11:50:51 > 42.651
19. Integer to Time (sy-uzeit + 3600 sec) = Seconds from 00:00:00): 46.251 > 12:50:51
20. Date Difference (sy-datum) - '20240101'): 354
21. Packed(1.23) to Character3: 1.2
22. String to UTC long: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_DATE_TIME exception
23. String (20241220) to UTC long: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_DATE_TIME exception
24. Character3 to UTC long: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_DATE_TIME exception
25. Date can't be converted to UTC long
26. Time can't be converted to UTC long
27. Number can't be converted to UTC long
28. Integer can't be converted to UTC long
29. Packed can't be converted to UTC long
30. System UTC time: 20.12.2024 06:20:51,5742130

Happy Coding!

entry.title: Managing Event Volume: Summary Events and Grouping Events in SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
The proactive resolution of integration issues is crucial for maintaining business continuity in a distributed yet interconnected customer landscape. Customers can define events in Integration and Exception Monitoring in  SAP Cloud ALM to trigger notifications or alerts whenever integration issues with specific statuses, such as "Error," are detected.

For instance, an event can be created for every error message, each of which corresponds to a business transaction that is impacted. However, it is essential to maintain a manageable number of events. When the volume of events becomes too high, it becomes challenging to prioritize and address them efficiently, which can delay issue resolution.

In cases of systemic issues, there may be a surge in error messages, leading to an event flooding situation that requires effective management and affect resource utilization. Using summary events and grouping of events within Integration and Exception Monitoring, customers can help control such event flooding, ensuring smoother and more efficient event management.

Refer the blog for more information on various events configuration parameters for the event ‘Issues Detected’ in Integration and Exception Monitoring in SAP Cloud ALM.

Summary Event

Customer Need:

Suppose the customer wishes to set up an event under the following conditions:

  •  All messages with a status of FAILED, ESCALATED, or PROCESSING.
  •  Messages that remain in the specified status for more than 15 minutes.
  •  An event should be generated for every individual message that meets these conditions.
  • No more than 10 events should be generated during each calculation period (5 minutes).
  • Email notifications should be sent to specific users.

Customer Configuration:

To fulfil the above requirement, the customer can set up the metric "Issues Detected" with the following configuration:

  • In the Filter section:
    • Remove the prefilled values for the status group and add the required values for status: FAILED.
    • Note: "Status" refers to the status of messages in the local cloud service. The status group maps these statuses to a specific predefined status group in SAP Cloud ALM. For example, the FAILED status in Cloud Integration is mapped to the Status Group "Error" in SAP Cloud ALM.
  • In the Parameter section:
    •  Set "Messages older than n minutes" to 15 minutes.
    •  Set "Number of events per calculation" to 10.
  • In the Grouping section:
    • Configure the parameters to include "Artifact Name", "Status", "Status Text", and "Message ID".
  • In the Event Action section:
    • Enable the email notification option and add the recipient emails.

How the configuration unfolds:

Now let’s take an example where we have 12 messages with a FAILED status in one monitoring data collection.

  • According to the configuration, the application will search for all messages that have been in the FAILED status for more than 15 minutes. 12 messages are detected.
  • The application will then group these messages as per the configuration. Since the grouping includes the Message ID as a parameter, and each message has a unique Message ID, we will end up with 12 distinct events.
  • Given that the number of events (12) is greater than the "number of events per calculation" (10), 1 summary event will be triggered, and all messages will be mapped to this summary event.
  • The user will receive 1 summary email, with 12 message details contributing to the event.
  • Sample email screen shot is given below for reference.

 

Grouping of Events

Customer Need:

Suppose the customer wishes to set up an event-raising configuration under the following conditions:

  • All messages with a status of FAILED, ESCALATED, or PROCESSING.
  • Messages that remain in the specified status for more than 15 minutes.
  • Events should be grouped by “Artifact Name” and “Status Text” for each relevant message.
  • No more than 10 events should be generated during each calculation period (5 minutes).
  • Email notifications should be sent to specific users.

Customer Configuration:

The customer can keep all the configurations as the previous example. Adjust the configuration in grouping section to reflect the grouping preference.

To fulfil the above requirement, the customer can set up the metric "Issues Detected" with the following configuration:

  • In the Filter section:
    • Remove the prefilled values for the status group and add the required values for status: FAILED.
    • Note: "Status" refers to the status of messages in the local cloud service. The status group maps these statuses to a specific predefined status group in SAP Cloud ALM. For example, the FAILED status in Cloud Integration is mapped to the Status Group "Error" in SAP Cloud ALM.
  • In the Parameter section:
    •  Set "Messages older than n minutes" to 15 minutes.
    •  Set "Number of events per calculation" to 10.
  • In the Grouping section:
    • Configure the parameters to include "Artifact Name", "Status Text".
  • In the Event Action section:
    • Enable the email notification option and add the recipient emails.
    • Create Alerts

How the configuration unfolds:

 

Now let’s take an example where we have 12 messages with a FAILED status in one monitoring data collection. Some messages for “Artifact Name=Replicate Business Partner from SAP Business Suite” and some for “Artifact Name =Confirm Business Partner Replication from SAP Business Suite”.

  • According to the configuration, the application will search for all messages that have been in the FAILED status for more than 15 minutes.12 messages are detected.
  • The application will then group these messages as per the configuration. Since the grouping parameters include "Artifact Name" and "Status Text," and all contributing messages having the same values for these parameters will be grouped.
  • We have 2 groups created.
    • Group 1: “Artifact Name = Replicate Business Partner from SAP Business Suite” and “Status Text= Inbound processing in endpoint at /MDM/C4C/ReplicateBusinessPartner failed with message "Fault:Unsup...”.
    • Group 2: “Artifact Name = Confirm Business Partner Replication from SAP Business Suite” and “Status Text = Inbound processing in endpoint at /MDM/C4C/BusinessPartnerSUITEBulkReplicateConfirmation_Out failed..”
  •  The user will receive 2 emails, one for each group and containing details of the messages contributing to this group. Group 1 will show 4 messages and group 2 will show 8 messages.
  • The user will also receive 2 alerts in Alert Inbox in Integration and Exception monitoring, 1 alert for each group. Alert for group 1 will display 4 messages and alert for group 2 will display 8 messages.

For more information on how to configure these settings, refer to Setting Up Events in SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Stop Guessing! Use Case with Conversational AI Agents in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

Have you ever stared at your analytics dashboard, wishing it could do more—like answer your questions without having to solely search, filter, and drill into your data? Well, guess what – you can do that now!

In this blog, I’ll walk you through an exciting use case that leverages SAP AI Core with SAP’s AI Agent through the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) in the following order:

  1. ‘Stop Guessing!’ Use Case – Business View
  2. What are AI Agents?
  3. Why SAP AI Agents?
  4. ‘Stop Guessing!’ Use Case – Technical View
  5. How can you get your hands on this?

 

1. ‘Stop Guessing!’ Use Case – Business View

Imagine you’re a business user at a Gas Provider Company, exploring your Finance and Billing Dashboards. As you dive into the billing data, a critical insight catches your eye: late fees are unusually high in a region. This sparks concern about potential customer dissatisfaction and retention issues. Here’s where the power of AI shines. By leveraging a Conversational AI Agent directly within your SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) dashboard, you can uncover the underlying trends, identify contributing economic factors, gain actionable recommendations, and more – removing the need to “guess” your next steps.

Let me show you how a Gas Provider Company can use a Conversational AI Agent to accelerate decision-making and uncover deeper insights beyond the 4 walls of their Enterprise. Watch the 'Stop Guessing' Use Case Demo below!

As you saw in the video above, the business user was able to receive an output that combines their Enterprise data from the HANA database and External data from PDFs and websites. This blog will further showcase how this capability allows businesses to have insights beyond the data in their 4 walls resulting in realistic, reliable results to drive business decision making. In other words, customers are no longer limited in data and information. With AI Agents you have the possibility to leverage a multitude of tools thereby providing you with a competitive edge. In fact, Gartner has predicted that by 2028 one third of interactions with generative AI will use autonomous AI Agents to complete tasks (Source).

To further understand the value SAP AI Core Services bring with AI Agents, we must first understand what an AI Agent is.

2. What are AI Agents?

AI Agents are NOT chatbots, but rather they are autonomous applications powered by large language models (LLMs) that can integrate with applications and business processes to achieve complex goals. They can fall into two categories:

  1. Task Oriented – Agents can automate workflows and multi-step tasks by leveraging integrations such as with SAP Build Process Automation.
  2. Conversational – Enable natural, human-like conversations to help users gain deeper insights as seen in the use case.

 Agents achieve their goal by using multi-step planning, self-examination and reflection of work to propose improvements, tool usage, and multi-agent collaboration to split-up tasks and come up with a solution. Some of these concepts will be seen in more detail in the ‘Stop Guessing!’ Use Case -Technical View section.

For example, in the use case video, the Conversational AI Agent examined the prompt in multiple steps by calling on the data and tools. In parallel, it also performed reflection activities to continuously learn and improve. Although there was no multi-agent collaboration here, an example would be a specific scenario AI Agent such as triggering a workflow to automate PR creations for approved marketing promo campaigns or, perhaps, calling on another Conversational AI Agent that has PR information specific tools to achieve goals around answering PR related topics.

 Source: Agents is the most important BTP release this 2024

Source: AI108 TechEd Session Session Catalog - SAP TechEd 2024

 

3. Why SAP AI Agents?

The market for AI Agents is rapidly growing with innovations from OpenAI, Microsoft, Google and other contributors. What makes SAP AI Agents valuable?

  1. SAP AI Agents integrate your semantically rich SAP data and processes, ensuring that the meaning of your business is captured. In other words, SAP knows SAP the best as it can understand, integrate, and capture your data's Business Context (eg. Hierarchies, metadata, currency conversion, languages, security configuration) - providing meaning to the AI Agent to reduce hallucinations and removing the need of manual rebuild of your data’s business context.
  2. The AI Agent integrates with SAP AI Core services by collecting data and leveraging underlying large language model (LLM) within the AI Core. It is also leverages HANA's Vector Engine for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). This advanced setup minimizes hallucinations, delivering more accurate, reliable, and trusted results.
  3. By leveraging SAP AI Core services and other BTP offerings, this will allow for improved efficiency and collaboration across your integrated Intelligent Enterprise Portfolio. You can create enterprise-grade AI Agents tailored to your unique needs that integrate seamlessly with tools like HANA, OData applications, SAP Build Process Automation, and even external sources like websites and PDFs, while ensuring you are the decision maker at the center of it all. This ensures secure interactions with your business data, processes, and systems while adhering to SAP’s clean core principles.

 

4. ‘Stop Guessing!’ Use Case – Technical View

 

In this section, we will dive deeper in the Use Case and explore the use case architecture and witness a Lab Preview of the Project Agent Builder – the new approach to develop agent-based projects in BTP.

Use Case Architecture

In this use case, the SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) dashboard is leveraging data from the HANA database that has been further transformed and modeled in SAP Datasphere. With SAP Datasphere's native connectivity to SAC, the data maintains it's Business Context and can be consumed in real-time analytics. In addition, the Conversational AI Agent has been embedded into SAC as a custom application widget via Business Application Studio (and can also be embedded via SAP Build Code).

 

 

The AI Agent leverages 3 tools in this use case: Semantically rich data from HANA, Financial PDF documents, and the Gas Providers' Supplier Website. If required more tools can be added beyond the 3, allowing you to have that flexibility to achieve your goal and leverage Enterprise data with External data.

When a question is asked to the Conversational AI Agent in SAC, it sends this to the LLM and grounds it using the HANA Vector Engine & RAG while searching the tools for the appropriate data to respond back with. In addition, it performs muti-step planning to search for information using the 3 assigned tools to produce an answer while continuously reflecting for improvement.

Project Agent Builder

Project Agent Builder is where you can create your Conversational Agent and define the various tools and integrations to achieve your goal. Below you will see a lab preview screenshot of the Project Agent Builder. Upon creating a new Agent, you must identify the following:

  1. Description of the Agent’s expertise and goal with instructions to illustrate the Agent’s behaviour.
  2. Maximum thinking or multi-step planning steps it must take before providing a response.
  3. Selection of the LLM provider, which in this case is OpenAI GPT4o.

 

 To better understand how you can leverage the Project Agent Builder and how it operates in this Use Case, check out the video below!

 

5. How can you get your hands on this?

 

AI Agents are planned to be released as a BTP Service in H1 2025.

You can additionally leverage this BTP service with Joule Studio to extend beyond the core and collaborate between multi-agents for scenarios. Further roadmap innovations will also allow you to extend your AI Agent with SAP Build Process Automation to automate tasks.

Check out the links below for our AI Roadmap in Q1 and Q2 2025.

Q1 2025 Roadmap - New Generative AI Tool Kit with SAP HANA Cloud: SAP Road Map Explorer

Q2 2025 Roadmap for AI Agents for Advanced Business Scenarios:  SAP Road Map Explorer

Source: AI108 TechEd Session Session Catalog - SAP TechEd 2024

 Further information on AI Agents with Joule Studio can be found here.

entry.title: Peering Options for SAP BTP
entry.content:
Customers occasionally inquire about improving connectivity between their on-premise business systems and SAP BTP, as well as between SAP cloud applications. This document provides options and guidance for implementing such a scenario.

Scenario 1: Connection to One Cloud Provider Using Their Peering Product

The simplest scenario is when customers want to extend their bandwidth or reduce internet latency between their (on-premise) business systems and SAP BTP. For this kind of scenario, there is the option to utilize the peering products of the infrastructure provider that underpin SAP BTP (or the SAP cloud application in question). By optimising the connectivity to the infrastructure below SAP BTP or SAP cloud applications, these systems inherit the advantages of this architecture.

For the three major cloud providers that support SAP BTP, the options are as follows

  • AWS: the corresponding AWS product is called Direct Connect: https://repost.aws/knowledge-center/setup-direct-connect-vif
  • Azure:  the corresponding Azure product is called Direct Peering: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/internet-peering/walkthrough-direct-all
  • For Google Cloud, the corresponding product is called Direct Peering, as well: https://cloud.google.com/network-connectivity/docs/direct-peering?hl=en

All three of these products work in a similar fashion. They require a leased line from the on-premise datacenter of the customer to the respective cloud provider. In addition to the physical connectivity, Layer 3 routing should be implemented using the Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) to allow for control on both the customer’s and cloud service provider’s sides (see Figure 1).

(Figure 1)

Scenario 2: Connection to One Cloud Provider Using Specialized Peering Providers

Most companies utilize the services of specialized peering providers like Megaport, Equinix, or a similar company.

These companies enable customers to establish a single physical peering connection with the peering provider, which then allows peering with all cloud service providers. This simplifies the setup process by removing the need for individual peering with each cloud provider. In most cases, customers can manage their peerings with cloud service providers through self-service options (see Figure 2).

(Figure 2)

Scenario 3: Connecting to Multiple Cloud Providers

This more complex use-case is the peering of services which are deployed on two or more different cloud service providers. To implement this scenario, the options are to either set up the routing between cloud providers individually on customer premises or use the capabilities of peering providers, similar to option 2.

 

For the first option, the architecture depicted on Figure 1 would be required. However, this would need to be implemented for all required cloud providers. A BGP setup would then be established between routing instances to connect to the cloud service providers and announce the routes between them (See Figure 3 for reference).

(Figure 3)

For the second option, peering providers typically provide a more automated solution, often in the form of a "managed service".

Many providers offer products that enable such a setup, like Megaport’s Cross-Cloud or Equinix’ Fabric Cloud Router (see Figure 4). In these setups, the peering provider announces the routes it receives from one cloud provider to the other. This way, there is an additional hop on the peering provider when connecting from one cloud provider to the other. This approach provides a fully isolated connectivity between two cloud providers and allows full control over the bandwidth and latency imposed by the connection.

 

(Figure 4)
