entry.title: Unlock the Power of SAC Custom Widgets: Personalize Your Excel Exports
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

Welcome to my very first blog post!

In this post, we’ll dive into how you can elevate your SAP Analytics Cloud experience by harnessing the power of custom widgets to enhance Excel exports.

Many customers express frustration with the limitations of the standard SAC export functionality, and that's where our custom widget comes in.

Whether it’s dynamic template selection, advanced cell formatting, or image integration, this widget provides unmatched flexibility and control over your exports.

Built using JavaScript libraries, this solution goes beyond the basics, enabling you to create fully tailored Excel files that meet your unique reporting requirements.

Let’s explore the features, benefits, and implementation details of this innovative approach to customizing SAP Analytics Cloud.

 

Constructor Initialization

  • Attaches a shadow DOM.
  • Clones the template into the shadow DOM.
  • Loads an external script for ExcelJS.

 

 

constructor() {
    super();
    this._shadowRoot = this.attachShadow({ mode: "open" });
    this._shadowRoot.appendChild(tmpl.content.cloneNode(true));
    this._firstConnection = false;
    this.loadScript('https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/exceljs/dist/exceljs.min.js', () => {});
}

 

 

Load External Script

Dynamically loads an external JavaScript library and executes a callback when loading completes.

Note that with this method, you can integrate any JavaScript library of your choice.

 

 

loadScript(src, callback) {
    let script = document.createElement('script');
    script.type = 'text/javascript';
    script.onload = callback;
    script.src=src;
    document.head.appendChild(script);
}

 

 

 

 

Start XLSX Download

Initiates the download process by fetching data and creating the template.

 

 

 

 

async downloadXLSX(dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract, headers) {
    var fullData = this.myDataSource;
    this.createTemplate(fullData, dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract, headers);
}

 

 

 

 

Extract Data Properties

Extracts specific properties (dimensions and measures) from the data.

 

 

 

 

// Extracts properties (dimensions and measures) from the given data
    getProperties(data, metadata, dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract) {
      let item = data[0]; // Use the first row as an example
      let row = {};

      // Extract dimension values
      if (dimensionsToExtract.length > 0) {
        Object.keys(metadata.dimensions).forEach(key => {
          let dimensionId = metadata.dimensions[key].id;
          if (dimensionsToExtract.includes(dimensionId)) {
            row[dimensionId] = item[key] ? item[key].label : "";
          }
        });
      }

      // Extract measure values
      if (measuresToExtract.length > 0) {
        Object.keys(metadata.mainStructureMembers).forEach(key => {
          let measureId = metadata.mainStructureMembers[key].id;
          if (measuresToExtract.includes(measureId)) {
            row[measureId] = item[key] ? item[key].formatted : "";
          }
        });
      }

      return row; // Return the extracted row
    }

 

 

 

 

Generate Headers

Creates an array of headers for the XLSX file.

 

 

 

 

getHeaders(dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract) {
      let headers = [];

      // Add dimension headers
      dimensionsToExtract.forEach(dimensionId => {
        headers.push(dimensionId);
      });

      // Add measure headers
      measuresToExtract.forEach(measureId => {
        headers.push(measureId);
      });

      return headers; // Return the header list
    }

 

 

 

 

Format Data

Prepares data rows based on the specified dimensions and measures.

 

 

 

 

  return data.map(item => {
        let row = {};

        // Format dimensions
        if (dimensionsToExtract.length > 0) {
          Object.keys(metadata.dimensions).forEach(key => {
            let dimensionId = metadata.dimensions[key].id;
            if (dimensionsToExtract.includes(dimensionId)) {
              row[dimensionId] = item[key] ? item[key].label : "";
            }
          });
        }

        // Format measures
        if (measuresToExtract.length > 0) {
          Object.keys(metadata.mainStructureMembers).forEach(key => {
            let measureId = metadata.mainStructureMembers[key].id;
            if (measuresToExtract.includes(measureId)) {
              row[measureId] = item[key] ? item[key].formatted : "";
            }
          });
        }

        return row; // Return the formatted row
      });
    }

 

 

 

 

Set Column Widths

Configures default column widths and wraps text in cells.

 

 

 

 

setColumnWidths(worksheet) {
      const defaultWidth = 20;
      worksheet.columns.forEach(column => {
        column.width = defaultWidth; // Set default column width
        column.eachCell({ includeEmpty: true }, cell => {
          cell.alignment = { wrapText: true }; // Enable text wrapping
        });
      });
    }

 

 

 

 

Add Headers to the Worksheet

Adds headers with specific styles to the worksheet.

 

 

 

 

addHeaders(worksheet, currentRow, headers) {
      const headerFont = { size: 12, bold: true, color: { argb: 'FF000000' } };
      const headerBorder = {
        top: { style: 'thin' }, left: { style: 'thin' },
        bottom: { style: 'thin' }, right: { style: 'thin' }
      };

      headers.forEach((title, index) => {
        let cell = worksheet.getCell(currentRow, index + 1);
        cell.value = title; // Set header text
        cell.alignment = { vertical: 'middle', horizontal: 'center', wrapText: true };
        cell.fill = { type: 'pattern', pattern: 'solid', fgColor: { argb: 'FFFFFFFF' } };
        cell.font = headerFont; // Apply font style
        cell.border = headerBorder; // Apply border style
      });

      return currentRow + 1; // Move to the next row
    }

 

 

 

 

Add Total Row

Adds a row to calculate totals for measures and style them.

 

 

 

 

addTotalRow(worksheet, currentRow, headers, data, measuresToExtract, measureMapping) {
      const mapping = measureMapping.reduce((acc, entry) => {
        const [technicalName, readableName] = entry.split(":");
        acc[technicalName] = readableName;
        return acc;
      }, {});

      let totalRow = worksheet.getRow(currentRow);

      headers.forEach((header, index) => {
        let cell = totalRow.getCell(index + 1);
        const technicalName = Object.keys(mapping).find(key => mapping[key] === header);

        if (technicalName && measuresToExtract.includes(technicalName)) {
          const total = data.reduce((sum, row) => {
            const value = parseFloat(row[technicalName]) || 0;
            return sum + value;
          }, 0);

          cell.value = total; // Set the calculated total
        } else {
          cell.value = '';
        }

        // Style the total row cells
        cell.font = { bold: true };
        cell.alignment = { horizontal: 'center', vertical: 'middle' };
        cell.border = { top: { style: 'thin' }, left: { style: 'thin' },
                        bottom: { style: 'thin' }, right: { style: 'thin' } };
      });

      return currentRow + 1; // Move to the next row
    }

 

 

 

 

Add Image

Inserts a logo (Base64 image) into the worksheet.

 

 

 

 

// Adds a logo image to the worksheet at a specified location
    addLogo(workbook, worksheet, imageBase64, cellAddress) {
      const imageId = workbook.addImage({
        base64: imageBase64, // Base64-encoded image data
        extension: 'png' // Image file format
      });
      worksheet.addImage(imageId, cellAddress); // Place the image in the specified cells
    }

 

 

 

 

Create and Download the Excel file

Create and writes the workbook to an XLSX file and triggers the browser download.

 

 

 

 

// Creates and downloads the Excel file with data, headers, and styles
    async createTemplate(fullData, dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract, headers, measureMapping) {
      const rowData = this.formatData(fullData.data, fullData.metadata, dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract);

      const workbook = new ExcelJS.Workbook();
      const worksheet = workbook.addWorksheet('Report');

      let currentRow = 1;

      const logo = this.getImage();
      this.addLogo(workbook, worksheet, logo, 'F1:G5');
      currentRow += 6;

      worksheet.views = [{ state: 'frozen', ySplit: currentRow }];

      currentRow = this.addHeaders(worksheet, currentRow, headers);

      rowData.forEach((data) => {
        let row = worksheet.getRow(currentRow);
        row.values = [
          ...dimensionsToExtract.map((key) => data[key]),
          ...measuresToExtract.map((key) => Number(data[key]))
        ];
        row.height = 40;

        row.eachCell((cell) => {
          cell.alignment = { vertical: 'middle', horizontal: 'left', wrapText: true };
          cell.border = { top: { style: 'thin' }, left: { style: 'thin' },
                          bottom: { style: 'thin' }, right: { style: 'thin' } };
        });

        currentRow++;
      });

      currentRow = this.addTotalRow(worksheet, currentRow, headers, rowData, measuresToExtract, measureMapping);

      this.setColumnWidths(worksheet);

      const buffer = await workbook.xlsx.writeBuffer();
      const url = window.URL.createObjectURL(new Blob([buffer]));
      const a = document.createElement('a');
      a.href = url;
      a.download = 'Report.xlsx';
      a.click();
      window.URL.revokeObjectURL(url);
    }

 

 

 

 

 

Full Code

Javascript file:

 

 

 

(function () {
  // Create a template element to hold the HTML structure for the custom element
  let tmpl = document.createElement('template');
  tmpl.innerHTML = ``; // The template is empty for now; can be customized later

  // Define a custom HTML element called 'com-sap-sac-customwidgetexcelexport'
  customElements.define('com-sap-sac-customwidgetexcelexport', class CustomWidget extends HTMLElement {

    // The constructor initializes the custom element and its dependencies
    constructor() {
      super();
      // Attach a shadow DOM to encapsulate the element's DOM and styles
      this._shadowRoot = this.attachShadow({ mode: "open" });
      // Append the cloned template content into the shadow DOM
      this._shadowRoot.appendChild(tmpl.content.cloneNode(true));
      // Track whether the element has connected to the DOM for the first time
      this._firstConnection = false;

      // Load the external ExcelJS library asynchronously
      this.loadScript('https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/exceljs/dist/exceljs.min.js', () => {
        // Callback is empty, as no immediate actions are needed after loading the script
      });
    }

    // Method to dynamically load an external script
    loadScript(src, callback) {
      let script = document.createElement('script');
      script.type = 'text/javascript';
      script.onload = callback; // Execute callback when the script is loaded
      script.src=src; // Set the script source URL
      document.head.appendChild(script); // Add the script to the document's <head>
    }

    //Returns the base64 image
    getImage(){
      return "imagebase64..."; // specify here your image in base64
    }

    // Initiates the XLSX file creation and download process
    async downloadXLSX(dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract, headers, measureMapping) {
      // Get the full dataset (source not shown in this code)
      var fullData = this.myDataSource;

      // Pass the data and metadata to create the Excel template
      this.createTemplate(fullData, dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract, headers, measureMapping);
    }

    // Placeholder method executed when the element is added to the DOM
    connectedCallback() { }

    // Placeholder method for redrawing or refreshing the custom widget
    redraw() { }

    // Extracts properties (dimensions and measures) from the given data
    getProperties(data, metadata, dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract) {
      let item = data[0]; // Use the first row as an example
      let row = {};

      // Extract dimension values
      if (dimensionsToExtract.length > 0) {
        Object.keys(metadata.dimensions).forEach(key => {
          let dimensionId = metadata.dimensions[key].id;
          if (dimensionsToExtract.includes(dimensionId)) {
            row[dimensionId] = item[key] ? item[key].label : "";
          }
        });
      }

      // Extract measure values
      if (measuresToExtract.length > 0) {
        Object.keys(metadata.mainStructureMembers).forEach(key => {
          let measureId = metadata.mainStructureMembers[key].id;
          if (measuresToExtract.includes(measureId)) {
            row[measureId] = item[key] ? item[key].formatted : "";
          }
        });
      }

      return row; // Return the extracted row
    }

    // Generates a list of headers based on dimensions and measures
    getHeaders(dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract) {
      let headers = [];

      // Add dimension headers
      dimensionsToExtract.forEach(dimensionId => {
        headers.push(dimensionId);
      });

      // Add measure headers
      measuresToExtract.forEach(measureId => {
        headers.push(measureId);
      });

      return headers; // Return the header list
    }

    // Formats the data by mapping dimensions and measures for each row
    formatData(data, metadata, dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract) {
      return data.map(item => {
        let row = {};

        // Format dimensions
        if (dimensionsToExtract.length > 0) {
          Object.keys(metadata.dimensions).forEach(key => {
            let dimensionId = metadata.dimensions[key].id;
            if (dimensionsToExtract.includes(dimensionId)) {
              row[dimensionId] = item[key] ? item[key].label : "";
            }
          });
        }

        // Format measures
        if (measuresToExtract.length > 0) {
          Object.keys(metadata.mainStructureMembers).forEach(key => {
            let measureId = metadata.mainStructureMembers[key].id;
            if (measuresToExtract.includes(measureId)) {
              row[measureId] = item[key] ? item[key].formatted : "";
            }
          });
        }

        return row; // Return the formatted row
      });
    }

    // Sets uniform column widths for the Excel worksheet
    setColumnWidths(worksheet) {
      const defaultWidth = 20;
      worksheet.columns.forEach(column => {
        column.width = defaultWidth; // Set default column width
        column.eachCell({ includeEmpty: true }, cell => {
          cell.alignment = { wrapText: true }; // Enable text wrapping
        });
      });
    }

    // Adds headers to the worksheet with styles applied
    addHeaders(worksheet, currentRow, headers) {
      const headerFont = { size: 12, bold: true, color: { argb: 'FF000000' } };
      const headerBorder = {
        top: { style: 'thin' }, left: { style: 'thin' },
        bottom: { style: 'thin' }, right: { style: 'thin' }
      };

      headers.forEach((title, index) => {
        let cell = worksheet.getCell(currentRow, index + 1);
        cell.value = title; // Set header text
        cell.alignment = { vertical: 'middle', horizontal: 'center', wrapText: true };
        cell.fill = { type: 'pattern', pattern: 'solid', fgColor: { argb: 'FFFFFFFF' } };
        cell.font = headerFont; // Apply font style
        cell.border = headerBorder; // Apply border style
      });

      return currentRow + 1; // Move to the next row
    }

    // Adds a total row to calculate and display measure totals
    addTotalRow(worksheet, currentRow, headers, data, measuresToExtract, measureMapping) {
      const mapping = measureMapping.reduce((acc, entry) => {
        const [technicalName, readableName] = entry.split(":");
        acc[technicalName] = readableName;
        return acc;
      }, {});

      let totalRow = worksheet.getRow(currentRow);

      headers.forEach((header, index) => {
        let cell = totalRow.getCell(index + 1);
        const technicalName = Object.keys(mapping).find(key => mapping[key] === header);

        if (technicalName && measuresToExtract.includes(technicalName)) {
          const total = data.reduce((sum, row) => {
            const value = parseFloat(row[technicalName]) || 0;
            return sum + value;
          }, 0);

          cell.value = total; // Set the calculated total
        } else {
          cell.value = '';
        }

        // Style the total row cells
        cell.font = { bold: true };
        cell.alignment = { horizontal: 'center', vertical: 'middle' };
        cell.border = { top: { style: 'thin' }, left: { style: 'thin' },
                        bottom: { style: 'thin' }, right: { style: 'thin' } };
      });

      return currentRow + 1; // Move to the next row
    }

    // Adds a logo image to the worksheet at a specified location
    addLogo(workbook, worksheet, imageBase64, cellAddress) {
      const imageId = workbook.addImage({
        base64: imageBase64, // Base64-encoded image data
        extension: 'png' // Image file format
      });
      worksheet.addImage(imageId, cellAddress); // Place the image in the specified cells
    }

    // Creates and downloads the Excel file with data, headers, and styles
    async createTemplate(fullData, dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract, headers, measureMapping) {
      const rowData = this.formatData(fullData.data, fullData.metadata, dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract);

      const workbook = new ExcelJS.Workbook();
      const worksheet = workbook.addWorksheet('Report');

      let currentRow = 1;

      const logo = this.getImage();
      this.addLogo(workbook, worksheet, logo, 'F1:G5');
      currentRow += 6;

      worksheet.views = [{ state: 'frozen', ySplit: currentRow }];

      currentRow = this.addHeaders(worksheet, currentRow, headers);

      rowData.forEach((data) => {
        let row = worksheet.getRow(currentRow);
        row.values = [
          ...dimensionsToExtract.map((key) => data[key]),
          ...measuresToExtract.map((key) => Number(data[key]))
        ];
        row.height = 40;

        row.eachCell((cell) => {
          cell.alignment = { vertical: 'middle', horizontal: 'left', wrapText: true };
          cell.border = { top: { style: 'thin' }, left: { style: 'thin' },
                          bottom: { style: 'thin' }, right: { style: 'thin' } };
        });

        currentRow++;
      });

      currentRow = this.addTotalRow(worksheet, currentRow, headers, rowData, measuresToExtract, measureMapping);

      this.setColumnWidths(worksheet);

      const buffer = await workbook.xlsx.writeBuffer();
      const url = window.URL.createObjectURL(new Blob([buffer]));
      const a = document.createElement('a');
      a.href = url;
      a.download = 'Report.xlsx';
      a.click();
      window.URL.revokeObjectURL(url);
    }
  });
})();

 

 

 

JSON file:

 

 

 

{
    "name": "Custom Widget Export Excel Universal",
    "description": "Custom Widget Export Excelc Universal",
    "eula": "EULA",
    "vendor": "SAP",
    "license": "",
    "id": "com.sap.sac.customwidgetexcelexport",
    "newInstancePrefix": "customwidgetexcelexport",
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "icon": "",
    "webcomponents": [
        {
            "kind": "main",
            "tag": "com-sap-sac-customwidgetexcelexport",
            "url": "/stdExport.js",
            "integrity": "",
            "ignoreIntegrity": true
        }
    ],
    "properties": {},
    "dataBindings": {
        "myDataSource": {
            "feeds": [
                {
                    "id": "dimensions",
                    "description": "Dimensions",
                    "type": "dimension"
                },
                {
                    "id": "measures",
                    "description": "Measures",
                    "type": "mainStructureMember"
                }
            ]
        }
    },
    "methods": {
        "downloadXLSX": {
            "returnType": "",
            "description": "",
            "parameters": [
                {"name": "dimensionsToExtract", "type": "string[]"},
                {"name": "measuresToExtract", "type": "string[]"},
                {"name": "headers", "type": "string[]"},
                {"name": "measureMapping", "type": "string[]"}
            ]
        }
    },
    "events": {}
}

 

 

 

 

Sample of usage

Add the custom widget to your story. Since the widget lacks a graphical component, it will appear as a transparent object.

Next, as the custom widget uses Data Bindings, attach the model from which you want to fetch data, and specify the measures and dimensions to include in the dataset.

Attach below script to an event (a button click or others) to trigger the export:

 

 

 

var dim = ["Version","Item","Date","Person"]; //Technical names
var measures = ["[Account].[parentId].&[Quantity]","[Account].[parentId].&[Price]"]; //Technical names
var headers = ["Category","Item","Date","Person","Quantity","Price"]; //Headers you want to show as table header in the Excel file
var measureMapping = [
  "[Account].[parentId].&[Quantity]:Quantity",
  "[Account].[parentId].&[Price]:Price"
]; //mapping between the measure technical name and the name used as table header
customwidgetexcelexport_1.downloadXLSX(dim,measures,headers,measureMapping);

 

 

 

 

Conclusion

In conclusion, this custom widget offers a flexible and powerful way to enhance your SAP Analytics Cloud Excel exports. While this is not a final version, it serves as a foundation you can build upon to meet your specific needs. Whether it’s adding colors, images, or hyperlinks, you have the freedom to design your Excel templates exactly as you envision. The possibilities for customization are virtually limitless, allowing you to create exports that align perfectly with your reporting requirements.

See you in the next blog! 😊

entry.title: Convert Manually Created Append Fields to Real Key User Custom Fields
entry.content:
Convert Manually Created Append Fields to Real Key User Custom Fields

Have you ever wondered how to convert your manually created "old" append fields into real key user custom fields? Doing so lets you easily expose these fields to OData services, SOAP services, SAP GUI transactions, and more, utilizing the same feature set. Have you ever wondered how to do that without losing your data and preserving your old field name, so that you don’t have to adapt your extension coding which consumes this field?

If your answer is yes, then this blog post is just for you.

Please note that this blog does exclusively address SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise and doesn’t address SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.
In this blog we use S/4HANA as an abbreviation for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise. In addition, for key user custom fields we use the abbreviation custom fields.

This blog post covers the following topics:

  • In which cases you can convert your manually created append fields and
  • How to convert your manually created append fields to custom fields

Custom field technology supports the conversion of manually created append fields to custom fields using transaction SCFD_EUI. These types of custom fields are called ABAP Managed Fields (abbreviation AMF).
This is especially helpful in the following two cases:

  1. You already have a manually created append in the application database table. Due to the high migration effort needed otherwise, you need to keep this field. However, you also have the requirement to bring this field into Fiori UIs, APIs, or analytical applications using the Custom Fields Fiori app.
  2. You have specific requirements regarding the data element of your custom field, which is not supported when using the Custom Fields Fiori app:
    1. Either you need a specific conversion exit on that field or
    2. You need to use a specific check table, which is then used in SAP GUI transactions, for example

Please note that whenever possible, you should use standard custom fields instead of ABAP Managed Fields. Using ABAP Managed Fields significantly increases complexity, especially in software logistics. We will dive into the details of this issue later in this blog post.

How do you enable an append field for the Custom Fields app?

Let’s assume you have an old append field ZZVOUCHER_CODE at hand, which has been added to the extension include structure SDSALESDOC_INCL_EEW_PS. This structure is included in database table VBAK.
You need to preserve the data of this field because the effort of migrating data and custom code to a new custom field with a different field name is too high.
First, make sure that you are in your key user development system and client. In this client, the Adaptation Transport Organizer must be set up. Second, check that you’re using the correct apps for transport handling (also refer to note 2660797) to assure a correct setup for custom field creation. Further details regarding the setup are addressed in the blog post Key User Custom Fields in S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and On-Premise: Dos and Don’ts.

If everything is ready, open transaction SCFD_EUI. You will see the following selection screen:

Transaction SCFD_EUI: selection screen

In this selection screen, you can filter for database table and field name. An example: enter “VBAK” as database table name and “ZZ*” as database field name. If there are too many fields, you could also enter the entire field name ZZVOUCHER_CODE.
Please note that you get built-in documentation describing the details of transaction SCFD_EUI by clicking the Information button on the top of the transaction.

Transaction SCFD_EUI: resulting list of fields

Now, you see the list of fields that apply to the selection criteria, including your field ZZVOUCHER_CODE.
When you select ZZVOUCHER_CODE, a details section below the field list displays additional information for the selected field. Looking at the Field Status column, you see that the content is different for the listed fields. Whereas the status for field ZZVOUCHER_CODE is Can be Enabled, there are also other values possible. All of these are documented and explained here: Enabling Custom Database Fields for Usage in SAP Fiori Applications.

The next step is to click Enable in the button bar on the top. Now, the pop-up Enable Fields for Use in App Custom Fields and Logic is displayed:

Transaction SCFD_EUI: Enabling an append field

The pop-up allows you to specify the Custom Field ID and other semantics of the custom field (in the screenshot, the value help for the Semantic Type has been selected). Since this needs to be a new custom field and not link to an existing one, we leave the default Enabling Type unchanged.
Note that in transaction SCFD_EUI you can’t influence the prefix of the Custom Field ID, as this must be the customer namespace you configured in the Adaptation Transport Organizer settings. This setting is done via the Prefix field in transaction S_ATO_SETUP.

The field ZZVOUCHER_CODE is character-like and should have a value help assigned. Therefore, we need to change the Semantic Type from the default Text to Code List. Please note that possible semantic types depend on the technical type. In this case, it could also be Email Address, Phone Number or Web Address.

As soon as you select Code List as semantic type, the Value Help View field becomes editable. You can either enter the name of an already existing CDS view or enter the name of new CDS view and select Generate right next to the Value Help View field. Clicking Generate will show a pop-up containing templates for both the CDS Code view and the CDS Text view. You can create the two respective views using ABAP development tools for Eclipse (hereafter abbreviated as ADT), copy the templates, and finally, replace the contained placeholders.

Note that both CDS Code view and  CDS Text view must strictly have the format given within the templates. Also, the CDS Text view must be language-specific. However, the template includes a comment with instructions for alternative actions.

In our case, both CDS views have been prepared in advance: the CDS view ZZVOUCHER_CODE_V as code view and the CDS view ZZVOUCHER_CODE_T as text view. The final data source of these CDS views is the manually created database table ZZD_VOUCHER_CODE. All three artifacts are shown below:

CDS Code View ZZVOUCHER_CODE_V:

 

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Voucher Code'
@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZZVOUCHER_CV'
@search.searchable
@ObjectModel.compositionRoot: true
@ObjectModel.usageType.serviceQuality: #A
@ObjectModel.usageType.sizeCategory: #S
@ObjectModel.usageType.dataClass: #CUSTOMIZING
@analytics.dataCategory: #DIMENSION
@ObjectModel.representativeKey: 'Code'
define view ZZVOUCHER_CODE_V as select from ZZD_VOUCHER_CODE
    association [0..*] to ZZVOUCHER_CODE_T as _Text
    on $projection.Code = _Text.Code
{
  @ObjectModel.text.association: '_Text'
  .defaultSearchElement: true
  key cast( code as ZZVOUCHER_CODE ) as Code,
  @ObjectModel.association.type: [#TO_COMPOSITION_CHILD]
  _Text
}

 

 

 CDS Text View ZZVOUCHER_CODE_T:

 

 

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Voucher Code'
@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZZVOUCHER_CT'
@search.searchable
@ObjectModel.usageType.serviceQuality: #A
@ObjectModel.usageType.sizeCategory: #S
@ObjectModel.usageType.dataClass: #CUSTOMIZING
@ObjectModel.dataCategory: #TEXT
@ObjectModel.representativeKey: 'Code'
define view ZZVOUCHER_CODE_T as select from ZZD_VOUCHER_CODE
  association [0..*] to ZZVOUCHER_CODE_V as _Code
    on $projection.Code = _Code.Code
  association [0..1] to I_Language as _Language
    on $projection.Language = _Language.Language
{
  .defaultSearchElement: true
  key cast( code as ZZVOUCHER_CODE ) as Code,

  @Semantics.language: true
  @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: '_Language'
  //Use this for language-dependent descriptions:
  //key <Name of Language Field in Data Source> as Language,
  //Use this for language-independent descriptions:
  key $session.system_language as Language,
  .defaultSearchElement: true
  @Semantics.text: true
  Description as Description,

  @ObjectModel.association.type: [#TO_COMPOSITION_ROOT, #TO_COMPOSITION_PARENT]
  _Code,
  _Language
}

 

Database Table ZZD_VOUCHER_CODE (base table of the views):

 

@EndUserText.label : 'Voucher Code'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zzd_voucher_code {

  key client  : mandt not null;
  key code    : zzvoucher_code not null;
  description : cfd_field_code_description;

}

 

 

After creating these CDS views, enter the name of the Code CDS view and click OK. This action carries out the enabling process. Make sure to have note 3355846 installed to avoid timeouts while enabling.

Upon confirmation of the dialog (pressing F8 or OK), you’re prompted to enter a package and a transport request for the objects, which are being created by the system to enable your field for the SAP Fiori app Custom Fields.

 Transaction SCFD_EUI: package selection

After the enabling process is finished, you can see all artifacts generated during the enabling process using the support transaction SCFD_FIELDVIEWER. There, double-click your new custom field ZZ1_VOUCHER_CODE in the list on the left side of the screen and then select the Enhancement tab on the right side.

Let’s assume that we also want to expose our field ZZ1_VOUCHER_CODE in the Fiori app Manage Sales Orders - Version 2.
Open the app and navigate to the details of one of the sales orders. Then, switch into the Adapt UI mode. Select the section (the Basic Data – Order Data section) where you’d like to add your ZZ1_VOUCHER_CODE field. Then, right-click and select the “+ Add: Field” option. Within the pop-up, select the “+” button in the upper right corner. As soon as you’ve done that, the Custom Fields app will be started with the context of the app to be extended.

In the Custom Fields app, search for your Voucher Code field and navigate to its details. Since the SAP S/4HANA 2023 release, you’ll get a pop-up asking if you want to enable the field for the correct service. In older releases, you need to click on the User Interfaces tab and within that, select the Services tab. Now, look for the OData entity with the description “Manage Sales Orders - Version 2” and enable the entry for this OData service or OData entity by clicking Enable Usage. Then, click Save and Publish to generate the custom field.

 Enable field ZZ1_VOUCHER_CODE for Fiori App “Manage Sales Orders – Version 2”

As soon as this is done, reload the Fiori App Manage Sales Orders – Version 2 and then redo the steps you did before. This time, when you click the “+” button, you’ll see the Voucher Code field in the list of available fields. Select the Voucher Code field and click Ok.

In case there are too many fields displayed, you can enter the field name in the search field at the beginning of the dialog.

 

Add “Voucher Code” field to Fiori App “Manage Sales Orders – Version 2” using Adapt UI

Activate this UI change by selecting the magic wand icon on the upper left corner.
Leave the Adapt UI mode and switch into Edit mode. Now, you’ll see the Voucher Code field on the UI, rendered as field with value help.

 Using the “Voucher Code” in the Fiori App “Manage Sales Orders – Version 2”

Are ABAP Managed Fields lifecycle-stable, and do they follow the clean core principles? These questions are addressed in the blog post Key User Custom Fields in S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and On-Premise: Dos and Don’ts (available soon).

Now, you are done! Congratulations!
With this, you’re now equipped to

  • convert a manually created append field to a custom field using transaction SCFD_EUI
  • know in which cases this is the right thing to do.

Please note that this blog has been elaborated by a team of experts. I want to thank Yasmina van Rooij (YasminavanRooij), Daniel Wachs (DanielWachs), and Georg Wilhelm (georg_wilhelm) for their extremely helpful contributions.

Feedback, Comments, or Questions:

You’re cordially invited to provide feedback, comments, or questions in the comments section below. Please follow our profiles to get updates when we publish more posts on these topics.
Feel invited to post and answer questions here (https://answers.sap.com/tags/270c4f37-c335-46e1-bfad-a256637d5e26) and read other posts on the topic (https://blogs.sap.com/tags/270c4f37-c335-46e1-bfad-a256637d5e26/).

Other related blog posts:

  1. Link to Dos and Don’ts blog
  2. How to implement a custom field of type “Code List based on CDS view” with a developer extensibility custom entity:  https://blogs.sap.com/2022/07/26/sap-s-4hana-key-user-extensibility-powered-by-embedded-steampunk-custom-field-with-abap-implemented-value-help/
    Also: “How to implement a custom entity in the ABAP RESTful Programming Model using remote function modules”   
  3. How to configure the value help as a context-dependent value help: https://blogs.sap.com/2022/07/27/sap-s-4hana-key-user-extensibility-custom-field-with-abap-context-dependent-value-help/
  4. How to configure intent-based navigation on a key user custom field of type “Code List based on CDS View”: https://blogs.sap.com/2023/06/06/enrich-sap-s-4hana-key-user-custom-fields-with-intent-based-navigation/ 
    and in this case, how to easily find the fitting semantic object and semantic object parameter: Custom field type “Code list based on CDS view”: Finding/defining the right value help view and the right Semantic Object and Semantic Object Parameter for Intent-Based Navigation
  5. Custom Fields in S/4HANA: How to control field properties like mandatory, read-only, and visibility
  6. Key User Custom Fields in SAP S/4HANA SAP GUI UIs: How to control field properties (mandatory,...)
  7. Key User Custom Fields in Fiori UIs based on SEGW OData Services: How to control field properties
  8. Key User Custom Fields in Fiori UIs based on OData Services exposed with Service Binding
  9. Custom fields in S/4HANA: Key User versus Developer Extensibility
  10. SAP Screen Personas: How to position key user custom fields exactly where you want them to be

entry.title: S4 HANA Graph Workspace with LangChain
entry.content:
Introduction

In today's world, data is extremely valuable, and being able to understand and use large amounts of information gives organizations a big advantage. Graphs are one of the best tools to help with this. They arrange data in a way that makes it easier to understand, explore, and analyze. This blog explores how to use graphs with Hana DB and GenAI features.

Prerequisites

  • SAP AI Core access and anthropic--claude-3.5-sonnet model deployed.
  • SAP HANA DB access.

The Core Components of a Graph

NODE: The node in a graph, represents entities like people, places, organizations, or concepts.

RELATIONSHIPS: The connections between entities that define how they are related or interact with each other.

ATTRIBUTES: Additional information about entities or relationships that provide further context.

SCHEMA: A formal representation of the knowledge within a specific domain, defining the types of entities, relationships, and the rules governing their interrelations.

Through this blog we will see how we can use HANA DB and Large Language Models to implement Graph which can provide with the answer to questions asked using natural language.

  • Create HANA DB graph workspace.
  • Insert data and data in vector format.
  • Generate the cypher query which is supported by SAP HANA Graph Workspace
  • Execute the query and get the answer.

Part 1 : Create Graph Workspace and Upload Data

For detailed explanation please refer this tutorial: Create an SAP HANA Graph Workspace | SAP Tutorials

 

Step 1 : Create two tables for Nodes and Edges of the Graph.

 

CREATE TABLE "ERP_NODES"(
	"NODE_ID"  NVARCHAR(5000) NOT NULL,
	"DESCRIPTION" NVARCHAR(5000),
	"NAME" NVARCHAR(5000),
	"NODE_TYPE" NVARCHAR(5000),
	"NODE_VECTOR" REAL_VECTOR,
	PRIMARY KEY (
		"NODE_ID"
	)
);

CREATE TABLE "ERP_EDGES"(
	"EDGE_ID"  NVARCHAR(5000) NOT NULL,
	"SOURCE" NVARCHAR(5000) NOT NULL,
	"DESTINATION"NVARCHAR(5000) NOT NULL,
	"RELATIONSHIP_TYPE" NVARCHAR(5000),
	"EDGE_VECTOR" REAL_VECTOR,
	PRIMARY KEY (
		"EDGE_ID"
	)
);

 

 

Step 2 :  Create Graph Work space

 

ALTER TABLE "ERP_EDGES" ADD FOREIGN KEY ("SOURCE") REFERENCES "ERP_NODES" ("NODE_ID") ON UPDATE CASCADE ON DELETE CASCADE ENFORCED VALIDATED;

ALTER TABLE "ERP_EDGES" ADD FOREIGN KEY ( "DESTINATION" ) REFERENCES "ERP_NODES" ("NODE_ID") ON UPDATE CASCADE ON DELETE CASCADE ENFORCED VALIDATED;

CREATE GRAPH WORKSPACE "ERP"
  EDGE TABLE "ERP_EDGES"
    SOURCE COLUMN "SOURCE"
    TARGET COLUMN "DESTINATION"
    KEY COLUMN "EDGE_ID"
  VERTEX TABLE "ERP_NODES"
    KEY COLUMN "NODE_ID";

 

 

Step 3: Download the data from here

Let's consider the following graph example for our case.

 

This excel contains sheets per node and edge. Node (Entities) sheet depicts Node with NODE_ID and Description.  Edge (relationships) sheet depicts how two nodes are related to each other.

 

Step 4: Upload the data using following code.

CODE:

 

import pandas as pd
from hdbcli import dbapi
import uuid
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.init_models import init_embedding_model
from gen_ai_hub.proxy import GenAIHubProxyClient
import os
from langchain_community.callbacks import get_openai_callback
os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_ID"] = "AICORE_CLIENT_ID"
os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET"] = "AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET"
os.environ["AICORE_AUTH_URL"] = "AICORE_AUTH_URL"
os.environ["AICORE_BASE_URL"] = "AICORE_BASE_URL"
os.environ["AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"] = "AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"
gen_ai_hub_proxy_client = GenAIHubProxyClient(
    base_url="AICORE_BASE_URL",
    auth_url="AICORE_AUTH_URL",
    client_id="AICORE_CLIENT_ID",
    client_secret="AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET",
    resource_group="AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"
)
embeddings = init_embedding_model('text-embedding-ada-002')
HANA_DB_ADDRESS = "HANA_DB_ADDRESS"
HANA_DB_PORT = "HANA_DB_PORT"
HANA_DB_USER = "HANA_DB_USER"
HANA_DB_PASSWORD ="HANA_DB_PASSWORD"
 
driver = dbapi.connect(
    address=HANA_DB_ADDRESS, 
    port=HANA_DB_PORT, 
    user=HANA_DB_USER, 
    password=HANA_DB_PASSWORD
)
cursor = driver.cursor()
# Read data from Excel
excel_file = "data_blog.xlsx"  # Replace with your Excel file path
 
customer_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Customer')
order_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Order')
product_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Product')
supplier_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Supplier')
category_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Category')
customer_order_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Customer_Order')
order_product_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Order_Product')
product_supplier_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Product_Supplier')
product_category_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Product_Category')

 
# Function to create nodes
def create_node(cursor, id, description,node_type, node_vector):
    query = 'INSERT INTO ERP_NODES (NODE_ID, DESCRIPTION, NAME, NODE_TYPE, NODE_VECTOR) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?))'
    cursor.execute(query, (id, description, description, node_type, node_vector))
 
# Function to create relationships
def create_relationship(cursor, id, source, destination, relationship_type, edge_vector):
    query = 'INSERT INTO ERP_EDGES (EDGE_ID, SOURCE, DESTINATION, RELATIONSHIP_TYPE, EDGE_VECTOR) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?))'
    cursor.execute(query, (id, source, destination, relationship_type,edge_vector))
 
# Create Customer nodes
for index, row in customer_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['ID']) and pd.notna(row['Description']):
        node_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query(row['Description']))
        create_node(cursor=cursor, id=row['ID'], description=row['Description'], node_type="Customer",node_vector=node_vector)

# Create Order nodes
for index, row in order_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['ID']) and pd.notna(row['Description']):
        node_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query(row['Description']))
        create_node(cursor=cursor, id=row['ID'], description=row['Description'], node_type="Order",node_vector=node_vector)
        

# Create Product nodes
for index, row in product_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['ID']) and pd.notna(row['Description']):
        node_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query(row['Description']))
        create_node(cursor=cursor, id=row['ID'], description=row['Description'], node_type="Product",node_vector=node_vector)
        

# Create Supplier nodes
for index, row in supplier_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['ID']) and pd.notna(row['Description']):
        node_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query(row['Description']))
        create_node(cursor=cursor, id=row['ID'], description=row['Description'], node_type="Supplier",node_vector=node_vector)
        
# Create Category nodes
for index, row in category_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['ID']) and pd.notna(row['Description']):
        node_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query(row['Description']))
        create_node(cursor=cursor, id=row['ID'], description=row['Description'], node_type="Category",node_vector=node_vector)

# Create relationships between Customer and Order
for index, row in customer_order_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['Customer_ID']) and pd.notna(row['Order_ID']):
        customer_places_order_edge_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query("CUSTOMER_PLACES_ORDER"))
        order_associated_to_customer_edge_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query("ORDER_ASSOCIATED_TO_CUSTOMER"))
        create_relationship(cursor=cursor, id=str(uuid.uuid4()), source=row['Customer_ID'], destination=row['Order_ID'], relationship_type="CUSTOMER_PLACES_ORDER", edge_vector=customer_places_order_edge_vector)
        create_relationship(cursor=cursor, id=str(uuid.uuid4()), source=row['Order_ID'], destination=row['Customer_ID'], relationship_type="ORDER_ASSOCIATED_TO_CUSTOMER", edge_vector=order_associated_to_customer_edge_vector)
        

# Create relationships between Order and Product
for index, row in order_product_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['Order_ID']) and pd.notna(row['Product_ID']):
        order_has_product_edge_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query("ORDER_HAS_PRODUCTS"))
        create_relationship(cursor=cursor, id=str(uuid.uuid4()), source=row['Order_ID'], destination=row['Product_ID'], relationship_type="ORDER_HAS_PRODUCTS", edge_vector=order_has_product_edge_vector)
        

# Create relationships between Product and Supplier
for index, row in product_supplier_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['Product_ID']) and pd.notna(row['Supplier_ID']):
        product_supplied_by_edge_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query("PRODUCT_SUPPLIED_BY"))
        supplier_supplies_product_edge_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query("SUPPLIER_SUPPLIES_PRODUCT"))
        create_relationship(cursor=cursor, id=str(uuid.uuid4()), source=row['Product_ID'], destination=row['Supplier_ID'], relationship_type="PRODUCT_SUPPLIED_BY", edge_vector=product_supplied_by_edge_vector)
        create_relationship(cursor=cursor, id=str(uuid.uuid4()), source=row['Supplier_ID'], destination=row['Product_ID'], relationship_type="SUPPLIER_SUPPLIES_PRODUCT", edge_vector=supplier_supplies_product_edge_vector)
      
# Create relationships between Product and Category
for index, row in product_category_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['Product_ID']) and pd.notna(row['Category_ID']):
        product_belongs_to_category_edge_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query("PRODUCT_BELONGS_TO_CATEGORY"))
        category_has_products_edge_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query("CATEGORY_HAS_PRODUCTS"))
        create_relationship(cursor=cursor, id=str(uuid.uuid4()), source=row['Product_ID'], destination=row['Category_ID'], relationship_type="PRODUCT_BELONGS_TO_CATEGORY", edge_vector=product_belongs_to_category_edge_vector)
        create_relationship(cursor=cursor, id=str(uuid.uuid4()), source=row['Category_ID'], destination=row['Product_ID'], relationship_type="CATEGORY_HAS_PRODUCTS", edge_vector=category_has_products_edge_vector)

cursor.close()

 

 

Part 2 : Generate cypher query from English language and fetching data from database

Let’s take a look at the problems we will have to address to generate cypher query from English language.

  1. Generate cypher query using Large Language Model
  2. How to identify unspecified data from query

Generate cypher query using Large Language Model

Prompt is helpful in generating cypher query from English language. We will have to provide the rules and schema to the LLM to generate schema.

SCHEMA:

 

[
	{ Node:  
		[  
		{ name:NODE_TYPE, data_type: STRING }
		{ name:DESCRIPTION, data_type: STRING } ,   
		{ name:NODE_ID, data_type: STRING }    
		predefined list of NODE_TYPE:[ 
							Customer, 
							Order,
							Product, 
							Category,   
							Supplier
							]
		]   
	}
]

Relationships: 
[ 
	{ CUSTOMER_PLACES_ORDER: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Customer), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Order) ], description : ' Customer places one or many orders '}, 
	{ ORDER_ASSOCIATED_TO_CUSTOMER: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Order), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Customer) ], description : ' Order is associated with Customer'}, 
	{ ORDER_HAS_PRODUCTS : [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Order), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Product) ], description : ' Order contains one or more products '}, 
	{ PRODUCT_SUPPLIED_BY: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Product), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Supplier) ], description : ' Product is supplied by one or more suppliers '}, 
	{ SUPPLIER_SUPPLIES_PRODUCT: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Supplier), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Product) ], description : ' Product is supplied by one or more suppliers }, 
	{ PRODUCT_BELONGS_TO_CATEGORY: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Product), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Category) ], description : ' Product belongs to a specific category '},
	{ CATEGORY_HAS_PRODUCTS: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Category), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Product) ], description : ' Category has one or more product '},
]

 

PROMPT:

 

You need to check all the relationships before generating query. Relationships and query may not have exact wordings. 
RULES: 
1. For the generation of open cypher query use node fields NODE_ID and DESCRIPTION in condition of NODE and generate cypher query only using provided relationships and schema.
2. There can be chances where, to reach final output one need to traverse multiple nodes.
3.Output can have multiple nodes.
4. Bidirectional edge Traversal means source node to destination node and destination to source can be traversed. Uni directional edge traversal means only source to destination can be traversed but destination to source cannot be traversed. Please check edge traversal during generating relationships in cypher query
5. DO NOT write Node Type in Match instead give it in WHERE clause.
6. In RETURN statement always give appropriate alias for row.
7. For COUNT and aggregator function always return with attribute of NODE Eg. COUNT(q.NODE_ID).
8. MOST IMPORTANT RULE: Strictly use one or multiple different relations in MATCH clause as shown if needed.\n Eg. \"MATCH (q)-[e1]->(a), (a)-[e2]->(p)\" .
9.Generated Cypher query should follow given schema and specified relationships only.
10. If in query user asks for tasks, return name of tasks. If in query user asks for Qgate, return name of Qgate. If query does not contains any of the node type or relationship type for output node, return \"\".
11. If user asks question(s) replace it with Qgate.
12.If exact data is provided in user question generate query. If data provided is ambigous and  node type for given data is not menyioned do not generate the query, only return unspecified_data. unspecified_data should contain explicitly user defined nodes and conditions in list.
13 Always give relationship value in WHERE clause for every relationship defined in MATCH clause. It should also contain list of data for which user has not provided NODE_TYPE or RELATIONSHIP_TYPE explicitly. Any data which is part of pre defined list of Phase and mentioned in user question, treat it as Phase.Predefined List of phases - [\"Prepare\",\"Deployment(Deploy)\",\"Realize\",\"Run\",\"Execute\"]
14. Do not generate the cypher query if no valid relationships exists between the nodes in provided schema.
15.Always analyze the whole schema and use shortest route and minimum relationships to reach output node.

Example of generated Cypher based on user question:
User question: \"What are the Realise phase Qgate questions for topic extensibility?\".
 The generated cypher should follow this query syntax : 
\" MATCH (p)-[e1]->(t), (t)-[e2]->(q) WHERE (p.DESCRIPTION = 'Realize' OR p.NODE_ID = 'Realize') AND p.NODE_TYPE = 'Phase' AND (t.DESCRIPTION = 'extensibility' OR t.NODE_ID = 'extensibility') AND t.NODE_TYPE = 'Topic' AND q.NODE_TYPE = 'Qgate' AND e1.RELATIONSHIP_TYPE = 'HAS_TOPIC_FROM_PHASE' AND e2.RELATIONSHIP_TYPE = 'HAS_QGATE_FROM_TOPIC' RETURN q.DESCRIPTION AS Questions\". 
Example for output_node:
User Question : \"Get all answers for extensibility\".
Here output_node is \"Answer\". Check which node user is expecting as output. \n\nExample for unspecified data :
User Question : Get all answer for clean core. Here, clean core is ambiguous, so it becomes unspecified_data. output_node is answer. Since clean core is ambiguous, generated cypher is empty and eventually io_node_information is also empty. 
Output :{\"cpyher_query\":\"\"io_node_information\":\"None\", \"unspecified_data\":\"[\"clean core\"]\",\"output_node\":\"Answer\"}
Instructions: DO NOT use any other relationship types or properties that are not provided in following schema. 
Schema: {schema}

The question is: {question}. 

Output Format : {output_format}
Do not include any explanations or details in your responses.

 

This is an example prompt. Prompt needs to be changed depending on need and models used.

OUTPUT FORMAT:

 

{
    "cypher_query": "Only Generated cypher query for questions",
    "io_node_information": "Every WHERE clause attribute used in query as key and value as JSON",
    "unspecified_data": "Data which is ambigous and it's NODE_TYPE can be multiple nodes from schema as python list of string engulfed in double quotes.",
    "output_node": "Return user's final ask which matches with NODE_TYPE in schema or else  return "None"."
}

 

 

How to identify unspecified data from query

Let’s understand the problem first.  Consider we get the query “What categories does Monica have?” or “What categories does John Cook have?”. Here we don’t know whether Monica, John Cook is Customer or Supplier. Now both can have categories of products. John Cook being a customer can have products from some categories in his order while Monica being the supplier she can have products form some categories to supply. To solve this problem, we have unspecified data in OUTPUT FORMAT. Here we ask LLM to identify the data which don’t have node type specified. In this case, John Cook or Monica is unspecified data. Now we make similarity search on unspecified data and find the NODE_TYPE of it and replace unspecified data in query with “NODE_TYPE + unspecified data”. So the new query becomes, “What categories does Supplier Monica have?” or “What categories does Customer John Cook have?. Now we again make a call to LLM to generate the open cypher query since the query will be more accurate. Also we have io_node_information in OUTPUT_FORMAT. This we will use to get the correct answer even if there are small typos in query. With the given attributes in WHERE Clause we make similarity search and replace the exact word or description available in database as open cypher uses string search instead of similarity search. output_node in OUTPUT FORMAT can be used to generate a nice descriptive answer. We can again use LLM to generate the answer by providing data retrieved from Database, query and output_node.

CODE:

 

import json, re, os
from hdbcli import dbapi
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.init_models import init_embedding_model
from gen_ai_hub.proxy import GenAIHubProxyClient
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.amazon import ChatBedrock
from langchain.schema import AIMessage, HumanMessage, SystemMessage

def replace_where_clause(query, replacement):
    for key, value in replacement.items():
        # Create regex pattern to find the clause to replace
        pattern = rf"({key}\s*=\s*')[^']*(')"
        replacement_string = rf"\1{value}\2"

        # Perform the replacement
        query = re.sub(pattern, replacement_string, query)

    return query

HANA_DB_ADDRESS = "HANA_DB_ADDRESS"
HANA_DB_PORT = "HANA_DB_PORT"
HANA_DB_USER = "HANA_DB_USER"
HANA_DB_PASSWORD ="HANA_DB_PASSWORD"

os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_ID"] = "AICORE_CLIENT_ID"
os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET"] = "AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET"
os.environ["AICORE_AUTH_URL"] = "AICORE_AUTH_URL"
os.environ["AICORE_BASE_URL"] = "AICORE_BASE_URL"
os.environ["AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"] = "AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"

gen_ai_hub_proxy_client = GenAIHubProxyClient(
    base_url="AICORE_BASE_URL",
    auth_url="AICORE_AUTH_URL",
    client_id="AICORE_CLIENT_ID",
    client_secret="AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET",
    resource_group="AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"
)
 
driver = dbapi.connect(
    address=HANA_DB_ADDRESS, 
    port=HANA_DB_PORT, 
    user=HANA_DB_USER, 
    password=HANA_DB_PASSWORD
)

chat_model = ChatBedrock(proxy_client=gen_ai_hub_proxy_client,
                        model_name= "anthropic--claude-3.5-sonnet"
                                     )

cursor = driver.cursor()
schema = """[
                { Node:  
                [  
                { name:NODE_TYPE, data_type: STRING }
                { name:DESCRIPTION, data_type: STRING } ,   
                { name:NODE_ID, data_type: STRING }    
                predefined list of NODE_TYPE:[ 
                                    Customer, 
                                    Order,
                                    Product, 
                                    Category,   
                                    Supplier
                                    ]
                ]   
                }
                ]

                Relationships: 
                [ 
                { CUSTOMER_PLACES_ORDER: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Customer), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Order) ], description : ' Customer places one or many orders '}, 
                { ORDER_ASSOCIATED_TO_CUSTOMER: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Order), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Customer) ], description : ' Order is associated with Customer '}, 

                { ORDER_HAS_PRODUCTS : [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Order), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Product) ], description : ' Order contains one or more products '}, 
                { PRODUCT_SUPPLIED_BY: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Product), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Supplier) ], description : ' Product is supplied by one or more suppliers '}, 
                { SUPPLIER_SUPPLIES_PRODUCT: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Supplier), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Product) ], description : ' Product is supplied by one or more suppliers }, 

                { PRODUCT_BELONGS_TO_CATEGORY: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Product), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Category) ], description : ' Product belongs to a specific category '},
                { CATEGORY_HAS_PRODUCTS: [ source: Node(NODE_TYPE: Category), destination: Node(NODE_TYPE: Product) ], description : ' Category has one or more product '},
            ]
"""
output_format = "{\"cypher_query\":\"Only Generated cypher query for questions. If multiple relationship exists, make sure to follow 8th RULE.\",\"io_node_information\":\"Every WHERE clause attribute used in query as key and value as JSON\", \"unspecified_data\":\"Data which is ambigous and it's NODE_TYPE can be multiple nodes from schema as python list of string engulfed in double quotes.\",\"output_node\":\"return user's final ask which matches with NODE_TYPE in schema or else  return \"None\".\"}"
query = "What categories does Monica have?"
question = query
cypher_generation_prompt = f"""Relationships and query may not have exact wordings. RULES: \n1. For the generation of open cypher query use node fields NODE_ID and DESCRIPTION in condition of NODE and generate cypher query only using provided relationships and schema.\n2. There can be chances where, to reach final output one need to traverse multiple nodes.\n3.Output can have multiple nodes.\n4. Bidirectional edge Traversal means source node to destination node and destination to source can be traversed. Uni directional edge traversal means only source to destination can be traversed but destination to source cannot be traversed. Please check edge traversal during generating relationships in cypher query\n5. DO NOT write Node Type in Match instead give it in WHERE clause.\n6. In RETURN statement always give appropriate alias for row.\n7. For COUNT and aggregator function always return with attribute of NODE Eg. COUNT(q.NODE_ID).\n8. MOST IMPORTANT RULE: Strictly use one or multiple different relations in MATCH clause as shown if needed. Do NOT combine two relations even though they are continous.\n Eg. \"MATCH (q)-[e1]->(a), (a)-[e2]->(p)\" .\n9.Generated Cypher query should follow given schema and specified relationships only.\n 10. If in query user asks for tasks, return name of tasks. If in query user asks for Qgate, return name of Qgate. If query does not contains any of the node type or relationship type for output node, return \"\".\n11. If user asks question(s) replace it with Qgate.\n12.If exact data is provided in user question generate query. If data provided is ambigous and  node type for given data is not menyioned do not generate the query, only return unspecified_data. unspecified_data should contain explicitly user defined nodes and conditions in list.\n13 Always give relationship value in WHERE clause for every relationship defined in MATCH clause. It should also contain list of data for which user has not provided NODE_TYPE or RELATIONSHIP_TYPE explicitly. Any data which is part of pre defined list of Phase and mentioned in user question, treat it as Phase.Predefined List of phases - [\"Prepare\",\"Deployment(Deploy)\",\"Realize\",\"Run\",\"Execute\"]\n14. Do not generate the cypher query if no valid relationships exists between the nodes in provided schema.\n15.Always analyze the whole schema and use shortest route and minimum relationships to reach output node.\n\nExample of generated Cypher based on user question:\nUser question: \"What are the Realise phase Qgate questions for topic extensibility?\".\n The generated cypher should follow this query syntax : \" MATCH (p)-[e1]->(t), (t)-[e2]->(q) WHERE (p.DESCRIPTION = 'Realize' OR p.NODE_ID = 'Realize') AND p.NODE_TYPE = 'Phase' AND (t.DESCRIPTION = 'extensibility' OR t.NODE_ID = 'extensibility') AND t.NODE_TYPE = 'Topic' AND q.NODE_TYPE = 'Qgate' AND e1.RELATIONSHIP_TYPE = 'HAS_TOPIC_FROM_PHASE' AND e2.RELATIONSHIP_TYPE = 'HAS_QGATE_FROM_TOPIC' RETURN q.DESCRIPTION AS Questions\". \n\nExample for output_node:\nUser Question : \"Get all answers for extensibility\".\n Here output_node is \"Answer\". Check which node user is expecting as output. \n\nExample for unspecified data :\nUser Question : Which products are associated with Michael Brown?.Here, Michael Brown is ambiguous, so it becomes unspecified_data as we don't know whether Michael Brown is Supplier or Customer. output_node is answer. Since Michael Brown is ambiguous, generated cypher is empty and eventually io_node_information is also empty. Output :\"cpyher_query\":\"\"io_node_information\":\"None\", \"unspecified_data\":\"[\"Michael Brown\"]\",\"ouput_node\":\"Answer\"Instructions:\n 1.DO NOT use any other relationship types or properties that are not provided in following schema.\n2.Do not assume NODE_TYPE of any data in question while generating open cypher query following above RULES. If NODE_TYPE is not mentioned in question, STRICTLY identify that data is unspecified_data.\n3.If no unspecified data then follow RULE no 8, STRICTLY.\nSchema: {schema}\n\nThe question is: {question}. \n\n\nOutput Format : {output_format}\nDo not include any explanations or details in your responses. The output shoud be parsable json object which can be passed to json.loads() in python."""
batch_messages = [
            [
                HumanMessage(content="You are an AI assistant which generates open cypher query using the given schema and its relations only if relation_ship exists between them.While generating query you double check it. Generate Open Cypher statement to query a SAP HANA graph workspace."),
                AIMessage(content= "Provide me the RULES and user question."),
                HumanMessage(content=cypher_generation_prompt)
            ]
    ]
resp = chat_model.generate(batch_messages)
resp = json.loads(resp.model_dump_json())
cypher_query_prompt_response = resp['generations'][0][0]["message"]["content"]
embeddings = init_embedding_model('text-embedding-ada-002')
# cursor = self.db.cursor()

response = json.loads(cypher_query_prompt_response)
attributes = {}
is_where_clause_replacement_needed = True
node_sql_query = f"SELECT TOP 1 *, COSINE_SIMILARITY(NODE_VECTOR, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) AS SIMILARITY FROM ERP_NODES ORDER BY COSINE_SIMILARITY(NODE_VECTOR, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) DESC;"
if type(response["unspecified_data"]) == str:
    response["unspecified_data"] = json.loads(response["unspecified_data"])

#User query has unspecified data.
if len(response["unspecified_data"]) > 0:
    for entry in response["unspecified_data"]:
        entry_CAPS = entry.upper()
        node_id_query = """SELECT TOP 1 * FROM ERP_NODES WHERE NODE_ID = ?"""
        cursor.execute(node_id_query, (entry_CAPS))
        query_res = cursor.fetchall()
        # Check if unspecified data has matching NODE_ID from our system
        if len(query_res) != 0:
            replacement = str(query_res[0][3]) + " " + str(query_res[0][0])
            # Replace unspecified ID with it's nodetype
            query = query.replace(entry, replacement)
        else:
            # Check if unspecified data has matching Description from our system
            entry_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query(entry))
            cursor.execute(node_sql_query, (entry_vector, entry_vector))
            node_sql_query_response = cursor.fetchall()
            if float(node_sql_query_response[0][5]) > 0.88:
                temp = ""
                temp = node_sql_query_response[0][3] + " " + node_sql_query_response[0][2]
                 # Replace unspecified Description with it's nodetype
                query = query.replace(entry, temp)
    new_question = query
    batch_messages = [
            [
                SystemMessage(content="You are an AI assistant which generates open cypher query using the given schema and its relations only if relation_ship exists between them.While generating query you double check it. Generate Open Cypher statement to query a SAP HANA graph workspace."),
                AIMessage(content= "Provide me the RULES and user question."),
                HumanMessage(content=f"""Relationships and query may not have exact wordings. RULES: \n1. For the generation of open cypher query use node fields NODE_ID and DESCRIPTION in condition of NODE and generate cypher query only using provided relationships and schema.\n2. There can be chances where, to reach final output one need to traverse multiple nodes.\n3.Output can have multiple nodes.\n4. Bidirectional edge Traversal means source node to destination node and destination to source can be traversed. Uni directional edge traversal means only source to destination can be traversed but destination to source cannot be traversed. Please check edge traversal during generating relationships in cypher query\n5. DO NOT write Node Type in Match instead give it in WHERE clause.\n6. In RETURN statement always give appropriate alias for row.\n7. For COUNT and aggregator function always return with attribute of NODE Eg. COUNT(q.NODE_ID).\n8. MOST IMPORTANT RULE: Strictly use one or multiple different relations in MATCH clause as shown if needed. Do NOT combine two relations even though they are continous.\n Eg. \"MATCH (q)-[e1]->(a), (a)-[e2]->(p)\" .\n9.Generated Cypher query should follow given schema and specified relationships only.\n 10. If in query user asks for tasks, return name of tasks. If in query user asks for Qgate, return name of Qgate. If query does not contains any of the node type or relationship type for output node, return \"\".\n11. If user asks question(s) replace it with Qgate.\n12.If exact data is provided in user question generate query. If data provided is ambigous and  node type for given data is not menyioned do not generate the query, only return unspecified_data. unspecified_data should contain explicitly user defined nodes and conditions in list.\n13 Always give relationship value in WHERE clause for every relationship defined in MATCH clause. It should also contain list of data for which user has not provided NODE_TYPE or RELATIONSHIP_TYPE explicitly. Any data which is part of pre defined list of Phase and mentioned in user question, treat it as Phase.Predefined List of phases - [\"Prepare\",\"Deployment(Deploy)\",\"Realize\",\"Run\",\"Execute\"]\n14. Do not generate the cypher query if no valid relationships exists between the nodes in provided schema.\n15.Always analyze the whole schema and use shortest route and minimum relationships to reach output node.\n\nExample of generated Cypher based on user question:\nUser question: \"What are the Realise phase Qgate questions for topic extensibility?\".\n The generated cypher should follow this query syntax : \" MATCH (p)-[e1]->(t), (t)-[e2]->(q) WHERE (p.DESCRIPTION = 'Realize' OR p.NODE_ID = 'Realize') AND p.NODE_TYPE = 'Phase' AND (t.DESCRIPTION = 'extensibility' OR t.NODE_ID = 'extensibility') AND t.NODE_TYPE = 'Topic' AND q.NODE_TYPE = 'Qgate' AND e1.RELATIONSHIP_TYPE = 'HAS_TOPIC_FROM_PHASE' AND e2.RELATIONSHIP_TYPE = 'HAS_QGATE_FROM_TOPIC' RETURN q.DESCRIPTION AS Questions\". \n\nExample for output_node:\nUser Question : \"Get all answers for extensibility\".\n Here output_node is \"Answer\". Check which node user is expecting as output. \n\nExample for unspecified data :\nUser Question : Which products are associated with Michael Brown?.Here, Michael Brown is ambiguous, so it becomes unspecified_data as we don't know whether Michael Brown is Supplier or Customer. output_node is answer. Since Michael Brown is ambiguous, generated cypher is empty and eventually io_node_information is also empty. Output :\"cpyher_query\":\"\"io_node_information\":\"None\", \"unspecified_data\":\"[\"Michael Brown\"]\",\"ouput_node\":\"Answer\"Instructions:\n 1.DO NOT use any other relationship types or properties that are not provided in following schema.\nSchema: {schema}\n\nThe question is: {new_question}. \n\n\nOutput Format : {output_format}\nDo not include any explanations or details in your responses. The output shoud be parsable json object which can be passed to json.loads() in python.""")
            ]
    ]
    # Again make the LLM call to generate the cypher query but with more enhanced data.
    resp = chat_model.generate(batch_messages)
    resp = json.loads(resp.model_dump_json())
    response = resp['generations'][0][0]["message"]["content"]

    response = json.loads(response)
    is_where_clause_replacement_needed = False

else:
    # If there is no unspcified data, make sure that whatever present in WHERE clause of generated cypher query has 
    # correct details as it STRING search to database.
    io_node_information = response["io_node_information"]
    if type(io_node_information) == str:
        io_node_information = io_node_information.replace("'",'"')
        io_node_information = json.loads(io_node_information)
    for key, value in io_node_information.items():
        val = value
        if "DESCRIPTION" in key:
            node_sql_query = f"SELECT TOP 1 *, COSINE_SIMILARITY(NODE_VECTOR, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) AS SIMILARITY FROM ERP_NODES ORDER BY COSINE_SIMILARITY(NODE_VECTOR, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) DESC;"
            value_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query(value))
            cursor.execute(node_sql_query, (value_vector, value_vector))
            attribute_result = cursor.fetchall()
            if float(attribute_result[0][5]) > 0.88:
                val = attribute_result[0][2]
        attributes.update({key: val})
if is_where_clause_replacement_needed:
    final_cypher_query = replace_where_clause(response["cypher_query"], attributes)
else:
    final_cypher_query = response["cypher_query"]
output_node = response["output_node"]


#Making a Graph Workspace query and retrieving the answer.
if final_cypher_query == '':
    print("Query not generated")
else:
    final_cypher_query = final_cypher_query.replace("'", "''")
    final_sql_query = f"""SELECT * FROM OPENCYPHER_TABLE( GRAPH WORKSPACE "DBADMIN"."ERP" QUERY ' {final_cypher_query} ');"""
    cursor.execute(final_sql_query)
    sql_query_response = cursor.fetchall()
    if len(sql_query_response) == 0:
        print("No data found")
    else:
        db_data = ""
        for row in sql_query_response:
            db_data = db_data + "-"
            db_data = db_data + str(row[0])
            db_data = db_data + "\n"

        final_response = db_data
        print(final_response)

 

 

NOTE: The prompt needs little modification depending on Large Language Model which is being used. The current prompt holds the ground with model provided by Claude AI (Model : anthropic--claude-3.5-sonnet).

entry.title: Key Success Factors for a seamless transition from SAP Business ByDesign to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public
entry.content:
Recently, we had the opportunity to hear from several customers and partners and their experiences transitioning from SAP Business ByDesign to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. Their stories contained valuable insights and lessons that we felt could help other customers navigate their own transitions.

Assess Current Challenges and Set Clear Objectives

Pinpoint specific pain points and ensure that these needs can be addressed in the new solution, beginning with a clear understanding and keeping these in mind encourages clarity in decision making. Scrutinise the scope items and their capabilities, in the Digital Discovery Assessment (DDA) and use the Fit-to-Standard (FTS) workshops in the Explore phase to ensure those objectives are met.

Choose the Right Implementation Partner

The choice of an implementation partner can make or break the project. The partner should have a deep understanding of your business and bridges the gap between technical requirements and organisational goals. Partners also provide expertise and dedication and are vital in creating a productive, collaborative experience which contributes significantly to project’s success.

Allocate Sufficient Internal Resources

It can be tempting to allocate minimal resources, to avoid impacting the business, but this can lead to delays or higher costs, further down the line, it is often reflected in the quality of the implementation. Customers noted that transitions require committed time and effort, especially as users balance ongoing work responsibilities with project roles.

Flexible Planning and Real-Time Adaptation

Flexibility in planning is critical during a project, unforeseen issues inevitably arise and willingness to adapt helps resolve challenges without derailing the process. There is a plan when you start, but like Norman Schwarzkopf used to say, "The plan is nothing, planning is everything". Flexibility allows course adjustment however, you should retain focus on your objectives described above.

Utilise SAP provided Accelerators

Accelerators are embedded in the Activate methodology, particular attention should be given to transition specific content, particularly the migration iFlows. The iFlows map and transform many of key master and transactional objects and can lead to significant timesaving, see these blogs for more details: Data Migration Accelerator: SAP Business ByDesign to S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition and Data Migration: SAP Business ByDesign to SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition (XML & CSV)

Making Go-Live Just Another Day

One of the customers described a principle of making go-live “just another day”, which centres around rigorous testing and change management helping to avoid stress when at system go-live.

A customer also noted that they did not consider go-live as the ultimate success milestone, instead, they mark success after the first month-end close, once they’ve run a full cycle and completed critical tasks like closing the books and submitting reports. At this point, we move into a partner led “hyper-care”, a support phase to address any post-launch issues and stabilise operations.

Final Thoughts

Every transition is a journey, and choosing SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition has opened pathways to scalability. With the right partner, resources, a flexible approach, and utilising SAP’s methodologies and resources, any organization can navigate this journey successfully. We hope these shared experiences serve as helpful guidance for others considering or undergoing such a transition.

Learn More from our Customer Transition Podcasts:

Episode 103: SAP Customer Transitioning Experience: SAP Business ByDesign to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Episode 111: Transitioning from SAP Business ByDesign to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: After Go-live with Felina and Snap

entry.title: Unified Overview of Status Events for Business Services
entry.content:
Life is full of surprises, where we enjoy the expected and embrace the unexpected. Concurrently, business processes in SAP Cloud ALM are also similar. Every scenario made of systems & services, will have planned maintenances and also may encounter unplanned disruptions & degradations.

For all of us, going through our daily work will be chaotic without support of tools like MS Outlook calendar. Gone are the periodic days of paper planners and diaries. Whether it is to prepare for a recurring status meeting or dynamically act to compensate absence of a colleague, these tools always come in handy. They ease our efforts with simplified integration, leading to improved efficiency and performance in our daily tasks.

Day to day work of business users will be equivalently easy if they have a unique functionality that transparently displays all existing planned and unplanned status events, along with past & future events. Just like a daily journal for your business services. Business Service Management application in SAP Cloud ALM Operations helps the user by managing business services and related status events.

 

User can easily

  • be prepared for maintenances
  • trigger actions to execute fixes for disruptions & degradations or
  • be aware of the information conveyed in the communication

The Event Calendar View in Business Service Management (BSM) provides this advantage to the user through simplified configuration and easily consumable user interface.

BSM not only provides Calendar View of status events but also feasibility for user to compare the Achieved Service Level Report of the Business Services against the assigned Service Level Objective. It will help user to evaluate their Business Service to take actions wherever required or remain assured that the it is at par to the expectations.

In addition, BSM pushes status events to relevant monitoring usecases so required actions can be taken for an unplanned events or suppress unnecessary alert for a planned maintenance.

With these features in BSM user can easily monitor status events in Business Services and plan a seamless course of actions accordingly.

#SAPCloudALM
