entry.title: Re: Condition Contract for Sales - Manual Delta Accruals
entry.content:
Hi @Miche1984, 

There are indeed several ways to create "fake" business volume (BV) to address adjustments in SAP CCM. Below are the options you can consider:

  1. Proforma Settlement Document:

    • This is a document created outside the CCM process and standard settlement transactions.
    • It doesn’t impact Financial Accounting (FI) but provides the necessary quantities for the BV profile.

  2. Billing Document:

    • You can create a billing document that doesn’t post to accounting.
    • This method is effective but requires close control to ensure these documents remain strictly for adjustment purposes.

  3. Custom Table:

    • Create a custom table to hold the data and information required to represent the BV.
    • While functional, this approach is less user-friendly, as analyzing data within a table instead of a document can be challenging for end users.

For any of the above options, you’ll need to configure a specific CDS view and/or leverage additional tables within the BV profile to ensure the extra BV is considered correctly.

Alternative Approach:
Another option, not mentioned in my earlier post but mentioned by Yordan, is to use the REBD condition type to adjust the business volume directly (rather than adjusting the rebate amount).

  • This requires similar maintenance effort as REJ1 and is primarily recommended for final settlements.
  • It avoids the recurring high effort of ongoing adjustments.

Each of these approaches has its pros and cons, and the choice depends on your specific requirements and system setup. Let me know if you need further details on any of these methods!

Best regards,
Gauthier Victor

entry.title: Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email in SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, or SAP ERP
entry.content:
In this blog, we will explore how SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, or SAP ERP can help businesses comply with the new German regulation by receiving electronic invoices by email. As of January 2025, a new legal mandate in Germany requires businesses to be able to receive invoices electronically from their suppliers. For more information about the law in general, check out this blog New B2B electronic invoicing law in Germany: What you need to know.

 

Multiple solutions available

While this blog focuses on SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP ERP, it's important to note that there are alternative SAP offerings for handling electronic invoices received from your supplier. To get a complete list, check out the Supported Business Scenarios.

For more information on some of these alternatives, please refer to the following blogs: 

  • Receiving Electronic Invoices Through the Peppol Network in SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition or SAP ERP
  • Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • Receiving Electronic Invoices Through the Peppol Network in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 
  • Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email in SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management (Planned to be published by December 31, 2024)
  • Receiving Electronic Invoices Through the Peppol Network in SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management (Planned to be published by December 31, 2024)

 

Overview of the process in SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, or SAP ERP

German businesses have two primary options to manage electronic invoices received by email.

Option 1: Automatic extraction, upload, and electronic document creation

  1. Receiving the Invoice: Your supplier sends an invoice or credit note to your SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, or SAP ERP system by email. 
  2. Extracting the XML File: The business system extracts the XML file from the email.
  3. Creating an Electronic Document: The business system creates an electronic document in the eDocument Cockpit or the Manage Electronic Documents app.
  4. Submitting for Validation: The next step is to submit the electronic document in the eDocument Cockpit or the Manage Electronic Documents app for validation to SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition. This step can be automated as well by using the eDocument Background Processing report.
    • Submit button in the Manage Electronic Document app
  5. Validating the XML File: The cloud edition validates the invoice and returns the validation result. It also creates an easy-to-read HTML version of the invoice and returns it to the business system.
  6. Review Status: You can view the new status after the validation as well as the HTML invoice in the business system in the eDocument Cockpit or the Manage Electronic Documents app.
    • Display HTML button in the Manage Electronic Document app
  7. Further Processing: If you’ve implemented an incoming automation solution, you can forward the invoice from the eDocument Cockpit or the Manage Electronic Documents app to the incoming automation solution for further processing. This step can be automated.
    • Create Incoming Invoice button in the Manage Electronic Document app

Option 2: Manual extraction, upload, and electronic document creation

  1. Receiving the Invoice: Your supplier sends an invoice or credit note to you by email. 
  2. Extracting the XML File: You extract the XML file from the email to your local system.
  3. Uploading the XML File to Business System: You use the Upload Tool for Incoming Messages report in SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition or SAP ERP to upload the file.
    • Upload Tool for Incoming Messages report
  4. Creating an Electronic Document: You use the eDocument Inbound Message Handler report in SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition or SAP ERP to create an electronic document that shows up in the eDocument Cockpit or the Manage Electronic Documents app.
    • eDocument Inbound Message Handler report
  5. Submitting for Validation: In the eDocument Cockpit or the Manage Electronic Documents app, you submit the electronic document for validation to SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition.
    • Submit button in the Manage Electronic Document app
  6. Validating the XML File: The cloud edition validates the invoice and returns the validation result. It also creates an easy-to-read HTML version of the invoice and returns it to the business system.
  7. Reviewing Status: You can view the new status after the validation as well as the HTML invoice in the business system in the eDocument Cockpit or the Manage Electronic Documents app.
    • Display HTML button in the Manage Electronic Document app
  8. Further: If you’ve implemented an incoming automation solution, you can forward the invoice from the eDocument Cockpit or the Manage Electronic Documents app to the incoming automation solution for further processing. This step can be automated. For more information, check Implementation of an Incoming Automation Solution.
    • Create Incoming Invoice button in the Manage Electronic Document app

For more information about the tasks you can perform in the eDocument Cockpit or the Manage Electronic Documents app for the electronic invoice you’ve received by email, see:

  • For SAP S/4HANA, or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition:  Validating and Processing Electronic Invoices Received by E-Mail Using SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, Cloud Edition.
  • For SAP ERP: Validating and Processing Electronic Invoices Received by E-Mail Using SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, Cloud Edition

 

More information

For details about the end-to-end solution, please refer to the following information:  

  • For SAP S/4HANA, or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, see:
    • Electronic Supplier Invoices (Receipt by E-Mail)
    • Integration
  • For SAP ERP, see:
    • Electronic Supplier Invoices (Receipt by E-Mail)
    • Integration

The integration of the Peppol Exchange process is one part of the integration steps for the end-to-end solution. If you are interested in why the Peppol Exchange process of the cloud edition is relevant for the receiving of invoices by email, check out this blog: Email Solution for Germany: Understanding Use of the Peppol Exchange Process in the Cloud Edition. In addition, we want to make you aware that if you already have done the integration of the Peppol Exchange process for the receiving of electronic invoices through the Peppol network, you don’t need to perform the implementation steps again to get to the email validation solution for SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition or SAP ERP.

 

By leveraging SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP ERP, businesses in Germany can seamlessly transition to electronic invoicing, ensuring compliance with the new legal requirements while optimizing their invoicing processes.

entry.title: Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
In this blog, we will explore how SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition can help businesses comply with the new German regulation by receiving electronic invoices by email. As of January 2025, a new legal mandate in Germany requires businesses to be able to receive invoices electronically from their suppliers. For more information about the law in general, check out this blog New B2B electronic invoicing law in Germany: What you need to know.

Multiple solutions available

While this blog focuses on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition for handling electronic invoices received by email, it's important to note that there are alternative SAP offerings for handling electronic invoices received from your supplier. To get a complete list, check out the Supported Business Scenarios.

For more information on some of these alternatives, please refer to the following blogs: 

  • Receiving Electronic Invoices Through the Peppol Network in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email in SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition or SAP ERP
  • Receiving Electronic Invoices Through the Peppol Network in SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition or SAP ERP
  • Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email in SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management (Planned to be published by December 31, 2024)
  • Receiving Electronic Invoices Through the Peppol Network in SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management (Planned to be published by December 31, 2024)

     

Overview of the process in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

The process of receiving electronic invoices by email in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition involves several steps. Here's a detailed breakdown of the procedure: 

  1. Receiving the Invoice: Your supplier sends an invoice or credit note to you by email. 
  2. Extracting the XML File: You extract the XML file from the email to your local system.
  3. Uploading the XML File to Business System: You use the Upload Incoming Messages app in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to upload the file.
    • Upload Incoming Messages app
  4. Creating an Electronic Document: You use the Inbound Messages app in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to create an electronic document that shows up in the Manage Electronic Documents app.
    • Inbound Messages app
  5. Submitting for Validation: In the Manage Electronic Documents app, you submit the document for validation to SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition.
    • Submit button in the Manage Electronic Document app
  6. Validating the XML File: The cloud edition validates the invoice and returns the validation result. It also creates an easy-to-read HTML version of the invoice and returns it to the business system.
  7. Reviewing Status: You can view the new status after the validation as well as the HTML invoice in the business system in the Manage Electronic Documents app. In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition further processing of the electronic document is not planned for invoices received by email. If you want to further process the invoice, use the solution Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email in SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management (Planned to be published by December 31, 2024).
    • Display HTML button in the Manage Electronic Document app

For more information about the tasks you can perform in the eDocument Cockpit for the electronic invoice you’ve received by email, see Validating Electronic Invoices Received by E-Mail Using SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, Cloud Edition.

 

More information

For details about the end-to-end solution, please refer to the following information: 

  • Electronic Supplier Invoices (Receipt by E-Mail) 
  • Validating Electronic Supplier Invoices Using SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, Cloud Edition

The integration of the Peppol Exchange process is one part of the integration steps for the end-to-end solution. If you are interested in why the Peppol Exchange process of the cloud edition is relevant for the receiving of invoices by email, check out this blog: Email Solution for Germany: Understanding Use of the Peppol Exchange Process in the Cloud Edition. In addition, we want to make you aware that if you already have done the integration of the Peppol Exchange process for the Receiving of electronic invoices through the Peppol network, you don’t need to perform all implementation steps again to get to the email solution for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

 

By leveraging SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, businesses in Germany can seamlessly transition to electronic invoicing, ensuring compliance with the new legal requirements while optimizing their invoicing processes.

entry.title: Receiving Electronic Invoices Through the Peppol Network in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
In this blog, we will explore how SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition can help businesses comply with the new German regulation by receiving electronic invoices through the Peppol network. As of January 2025, a new legal mandate in Germany requires businesses to be able to receive invoices electronically from their suppliers. For more information about the law in general, check out this blog New B2B electronic invoicing law in Germany: What you need to know.

 

Multiple solutions available

While this blog focuses on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, it's important to note that there are alternative SAP offerings for handling electronic invoices received from your supplier. To get a complete list, check out the Supported Business Scenarios.

For more information on some of these alternatives, please refer to the following blogs: 

  • Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email in SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition or SAP ERP
  • Receiving Electronic Invoices Through the Peppol Network in SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private E...
  • Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email in SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management (Planned to be published by December 31, 2024)
  • Receiving Electronic Invoices Through the Peppol Network in SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management (Planned to be published by December 31, 2024)

 

Overview of the process in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

The process of receiving electronic invoices through the Peppol network in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition involves several steps. Here's a detailed breakdown of the procedure: 

  1. Receiving the Invoice: Your supplier sends an invoice or credit note to you through the Peppol network. 
  2. Receiving the invoice in the receiver access point: SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition acts as the receiver access point.
  3. Validating the electronic document:  The cloud edition runs business and technical checks and returns a delivery notification to the sender access point.
  4. Creating an Electronic Document: The cloud edition forwards the invoice S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition where the electronic document is created in the Manage Electronic Documents app. Further processing of the electronic document is not planned for supplier invoices. If you want to further process the invoice, use the solution  Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email in SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management (Planned to be published by December 31, 2024).
    • Electronic Document in the Manage Electronic Documents app

For more information about the tasks you can perform in the Manage Electronic Documents app for the electronic invoice you’ve received by email, see Processing Supplier Invoices.

 

More information

To implement the end-to-end solution, you must:

  • have integrated SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition and have completed the onboarding activities. See Integrating Peppol Exchange with SAP S/4HANA Cloud.
  • have performed the required configuration activities. See Configuration Options for Key Users.

 

By leveraging SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, businesses in Germany can seamlessly transition to electronic invoicing, ensuring compliance with the new legal requirements while optimizing their invoicing processes.

entry.title: Receiving Electronic Invoices Through the Peppol Network in SAP S/4HANA or SAP ERP
entry.content:
In this blog, we will explore how SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, or SAP ERP can help businesses comply with the new German regulation by receiving electronic invoices through the Peppol network. As of January 2025, a new legal mandate in Germany requires businesses to be able to receive invoices electronically from their suppliers. For more information about the law in general, check out this blog New B2B electronic invoicing law in Germany: What you need to know.

 

Multiple solutions available

While this blog focuses on SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP ERP, it's important to note that there are alternative SAP offerings for handling electronic invoices received from your supplier. To get a complete list, check out the Supported Business Scenarios.

For more information on some of these alternatives, please refer to the following blogs: 

  • Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email in SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition or SAP ERP
  • Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • Receiving Electronic Invoices Through the Peppol Network in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 
  • Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email in SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management (Planned to be published by December 31, 2024)
  • Receiving Electronic Invoices Through the Peppol Network in SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management (Planned to be published by December 31, 2024)

 

Overview of the process in SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, or SAP ERP

The process of receiving electronic invoices through the Peppol network in SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP ERP involves several steps. Here's a detailed breakdown of the procedure: 

  1. Receiving the Invoice: Your supplier sends an invoice or credit note to you through the Peppol network. 
  2. Receiving the invoice in the receiver access point: SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition acts as the receiver access point.
  3. Validating the electronic document: The cloud edition runs business and technical checks and returns a delivery notification to the sender access point.
  4. Creating an Electronic Document: The cloud edition forwards the invoice to the eDocument Cockpit or the Manage Electronic Documents app in SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP ERP, where an electronic document is created.
    • Electronic Document in the Manage Electronic Documents app
  5. Further Processing: If you’ve implemented an incoming automation solution, the system forwards the invoice from the eDocument Cockpit or the Manage Electronic Documents app to the incoming automation solution for further processing.

For more information about the tasks you can perform in the eDocument Cockpit or the Manage Electronic Documents app for the electronic invoice you’ve received by email, see:

  • For SAP S/4HANA, or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition: Processing Electronic Supplier Invoices
  • For SAP ERP: Germany Incoming eInvoice 

 

More information

To implement the end-to-end solution, you must:

  • Have integrated the Peppol Exchange process for the cloud edition of SAP Document and Reporting Compliance and done the onboarding activities. See:
    • For SAP S/4HANA, or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition: Integrating Peppol Exchange with SAP S/4HANA
    • For SAP ERP:  Integrating Peppol Exchange with SAP ERP
  • Have done the customizing steps. See:
    • For SAP S/4HANA, or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition: Customizing
    • For SAP ERP: Customizing

By leveraging SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP ERP, businesses in Germany can seamlessly transition to electronic invoicing, ensuring compliance with the new legal requirements while optimizing their invoicing processes.

entry.title: Email Solution for Germany: Understanding Use of the Peppol Exchange Process in the Cloud Edition
entry.content:
For Germany, SAP offers multiple solutions to handle invoices received from your suppliers by email*, all leveraging SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition. In this blog, we'll clarify the role of the cloud edition in the process of receiving electronic invoices by email.

 

The Role of the Cloud Edition

In the end-to-end process, the cloud edition performs the validation of the electronic invoices. You might notice a difference compared to using the cloud edition for sending invoices through the Peppol network, where the cloud edition functions as an access point.

 

Integration

To actually use the cloud edition for the validation, you must implement the Peppol Exchange process. The detailed steps are described on the SAP Help Portal: Check out the Supported Business Scenarios, and find the right implementation steps for your business system in the Links to Integration Steps column.

 

*Here are links to blogs about the different solutions to handle invoices received from your suppliers by email:

  • Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email in SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition or SAP ERP
  • Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • Receiving Electronic Invoices by Email in SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management (Planned to be published by December 31, 2024)

entry.title: Zusammenfassung der SAP Change und Training Kaffee-Ecke - Lean Coffee Session: Rückblick & Ausblick
entry.content:
Am 19. Dezember 2024 traf sich die SAP Change und Training Community zur letzten virtuellen Session des Jahres. Dabei reflektierten wir die Learnings aus 2024 und diskutierten zukünftige Schwerpunkte für 2025. Die Inputs sowie Ergebnisse findet Ihr anbei sowie hier auf unserem Mural.

Warm-up und Eingangsfragen

Zu Beginn des Treffens teilten im Check-In alle Teilnehmenden ihre Lernerfahrungen aus 2024 sowie ihre geplanten Schwerpunkte für 2025 im Chat. Die Themen drehten sich primär um Künstliche Intelligenz (KI), effektives Prompting und Compliance Change Management. Erwähnt wurde im Rückblick auch das SAP Training and Adoption Forum und die Wichtigkeit, sich immer mal wieder in Person zu treffen.

Danach nutzten wir die Methode des Lean-Coffee für individuelle Rückblicke. Diese Methode ist ein strukturiertes, aber agenda-freies Meetingformat, bei dem Teilnehmende Themen selbst vorschlagen, priorisieren und in festgelegten Zeitintervallen diskutieren, um einen offenen Austausch und effektive Zusammenarbeit zu fördern. Fünf Personen aus der Community erklärten sich bereit, kurze Impulse zu Teilen.

Heike Senff (EY): Führungsverhalten für eine erfolgreiche Transformation

Heike betonte die wichtige Rolle der Führungskräfte in Transformationsprojekten. Ihrer Erfahrung nach sind Führungskräfte teils schlecht vorbereitet, da u.a. Verantwortlichkeiten nicht klar definiert sind oder die Kommunikation nicht optimal läuft. Außerdem würden diese eher den Fokus auf kurzfristige Ziele wie Budget und Deadlines legen, während die strategische Gesamtansicht oft fehlt.

Heike plädiert dafür, klassische Change-Methoden stärker mit Verhaltensbewertung zu verknüpfen und das Führungserhalten systematisch zu bewerten. Für 2025 plant sie, ein strukturiertes Framework zu entwickeln, das Führung und Verhalten in Transformationsprojekten messbar verbessert. Für die Anforderungen an Führungskräfte möchte sie u.a. die vereinbarten Werte oder Code of Conducts von Unternehmen nutzen.

Torben Mauch (Nagarro): User Adoption und Komplexität

Torben reflektierte, dass Projekte zunehmend komplexer werden und stets im Gesamtkontext betrachtet werden müssen. Er wies darauf hin, dass der Einsatz von KI aktuell noch nicht sein volles Potenzial erreicht hat, sie dennoch als wertvolles Hilfsmittel dienen kann und vor allem im Gegensatz zum Jahr davor endlich nutzbar ist.

Er warnte vor der Erwartungshaltung, dass Lernen ohne Anstrengung möglich sei und betonte die Notwendigkeit von Trainings trotz knapper Zeitpläne bis zum GoLive. Denn Lernen geschieht nicht mühelos, da es ohne eigene Anstrengung nicht funktioniert - auch wenn digitale Adoption Plattformen und Performance Support diese Anstrengung abfedern können. Für 2025 liegt Torbens Fokus auf den Themen von LeanIX, Signavio und SAP Enable Now/ WalkMe, wobei der Mensch bei allen Vorhaben im Mittelpunkt stehen soll.

Jasmin Rapp (Hugo Boss): Persönliche Impulse und Veränderung

Jasmin teilte ihre persönlichen Learnings aus dem Jahr 2024. Ein zentrales Learning war, dass Veränderungen oft einen Schmerzpunkt benötigen, um angestoßen zu werden, wie am Beispiel ihres Key User Prozesses bei Hugo Boss. Sie betonte die Wichtigkeit, regelmäßig das "Big Picture" zu betrachten und innovative Konzepte mit einem Plan B und Allianzen zu unterstützen: Wer mehr Unterstützende hat, könne sich in ihrer Erfahrung z.B. das Budget sichern, da nach der Umsetzung mehr Personen auch einen Nutzen aus dieser Innovation ziehen können.

Für 2025 appelliert Jasmin dazu, mutig zu sein und Veränderungen in der Welt anzustoßen sowie Rituale wie die Rauhnächte zu nutzen, um das Jahr zu reflektieren und sich auf das kommende Jahr mit Zielsetzungen vorzubereiten. Diese Rituale stießen in der Community auf großes Interesse.

Yvonne Paterno (SBB): SAPWise

Yvonne präsentierte SAPWise - einen SAP S/4HANA ChatBot, den die SBB entwickelt hat, um ihre User-Community besser zu unterstützen. Dieses Tool kann häufige Userfragen automatisiert beantworten und befindet sich aktuell in der Test- und Ausbauphase. Yvonne erklärte, dass sie im Laufe des Jahres festgestellt hat, dass Schulungsinhalte oft zu schwer zugänglich sind. Um die Anwendenden dabei besser zu unterstützen und auch effizienter zu schulen, wurde die Idee eines Chatbots geboren. Dieser soll ermöglichen, dass Anwendende Antworten auf ihre Fragen direkt innerhalb des SAP S/4HANA Systems bekommen.

Die SBB hat in Zusammenarbeit mit SAP eine Co-Innovation auf Basis der SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) initiiert. Während des Entwicklungsprozesses wurden verschiedene Maßnahmen umgesetzt:

  • Hackathons und Workshops: Mit End- und Key Usern wurden Workshops und Hackathons durchgeführt, um häufig gestellte Fragen zu sammeln und zu verstehen, welche Antworten nützlich sind.
  • Integration existierender Datenquellen: Der Chatbot greift auf bestehende Ressourcen wie SAP Enable Now, den SAP Companion und die internen Schulungsinhalte zu.
  • Live-Demo des Chatbots: Yvonne zeigte, wie der Chatbot funktioniert. Bei Bedarf leitet er Anfragen an die richtigen Expert:innen weiter und lernt dabei kontinuierlich dazu. Er ist so gestaltet, dass er Schritt-für-Schritt-Anleitungen und Verlinkungen zu weiterführenden Ressourcen wie Schulungsdokumenten und -videos bereitstellen kann.

Geplant ist, dass der Chatbot im Januar 2025 live geht. Yvonne berichtete, dass eine der größten Herausforderungen im Projekt darin bestand, dass der technische Support oft nicht nah genug am User ist, um dessen konkrete Bedürfnisse zu verstehen. Mit dem Chatbot hofft man, diese Lücke zu schließen.

Markus Waldl: Veränderungen in der Consultant-Rolle

Markus entschied sich spontan dazu, auch seine Learnings mit der Community zu teilen. Er diskutierte die evolutionären Veränderungen in der Rolle von Berater:innen, die zunehmend von reinen Expert:innen zu Begleitenden und Komplementärberatenden werden. Dieser Wandel erfordert neue Skills und eine offenere Haltung gegenüber Coaching und systemischen Beratungstechniken. Denn mit diesen Skills können Kunden eher mit Hilfe zur Selbsthilfe unterstützt werden, anstatt nur technische Probleme zu lösen.

Themen und Impulse für 2025:

Am Ende sammelten wir noch gemeinsam Wünsche für Themen die wir in der Community in 2025 behandeln möchten. Hier eine erste Aufzählung:

  • Verbindung von Change Management und Wissensmanagement: Wichtiger Impuls, um effektive Veränderungen zu unterstützen.
  • KI in der Wissensarbeit in SAP-Projekten: Nutzung z.B. von MS Copilot und anderen KI-Tools zur Verbesserung der Effizienz und Unterstützung in Projekten.
  • Neuroscience und menschliches Verhalten: Warum mag unser Gehirn Veränderungen nicht und wie kann dieses Wissen bei der Mitnahme und Befähigung der Nutzer helfen?
  • Prozess-KI: Bündelung des Prozesswissens in KI-Systemen, um bessere und informiertere Entscheidungen zu ermöglichen.
  • Mensch im Mittelpunkt: Sicherstellung, dass trotz aller Technologisierung und KI der Mensch weiterhin im Mittelpunkt der Entwicklungen und Entscheidungen steht.

Wir danken allen Teilnehmenden für ihre wertvollen Beiträge und freuen uns auf ein spannendes 2025 mit weiterhin regem Austausch und Eurer Mitgestaltung in der Community.

Weitere Informationen zur SAP Change und Training Kaffee-Ecke sowie kommende Termine findet ihr auf der hier verlinkten Seite. Die nächste Session findet am 16. Januar mit Anja Scheurer von SAP statt: Sie wird zum Jahresauftakt das SAP OCM Starterkit vorstellen – kommt gerne über diesen Link dazu!

Wir wünschen Euch allen frohe Feiertage und einen erfolgreichen Start ins Jahr 2025!

Euer SAP Change und Training Team

entry.title: CAP Version 8 Update: Issue with $batch Function Call Responses
entry.content:
Dear community,

We are currently in the process of updating the CAP (Cloud Application Programming) version in our product. During this transition to version 8, we have encountered an issue regarding the handling of $batch calls.

In our frontend implementation, we have two function calls that are executed consecutively. These calls are subsequently grouped into a single $batch request by SAPUI5. However, since the CAP update, we have observed that the results being returned from these functions are incorrect. Specifically:

  • The function “takeAuthors”, which is intended to return a list of authors, is instead returning the count of books.
  • Conversely, the function “booksCount”, which is supposed to return the book count, is incorrectly returning the list of authors.

CallResponse

I put a small timeout in a function to simulate processing some data, otherwise the anomaly does not occur.

The only reference I found in the CAP documentation is on the following page:

Noteworthy Changes 

To further illustrate the problem, I have created a GitHub repository containing a demonstration of the anomaly we are experiencing.

You can access it here: GitHub Repository - Test_CAP .

Any help would be very much appreciated

Thank you!
Kevin

entry.title: Re: Combing results from two cubes into one
entry.content:
It would help to have a sample of object names from each data provider cubes and how they relate to each other, but I'll attempt to answer.

In your report, you will need to MERGE the dimension objects that you would use to join the cubes.  Multiselect each object pairing, right-click, and merge.  Use these merged dimension objects in your report table instead of the dimensions from each cube.

All other objects that you wish to include in the report table, will need to be defined as measures or details.  If they are dimensions, then create a variable of type detail that is dependent on one of the merged dimensions and use the variable in your report table.

Hopefully, I guessed your issue correctly and explained this well enough.

Merry Christmas

entry.title: Seamless Navigation in SAP Analytics Cloud: From Dashboards to Business Applications
entry.content:
Introduction

In this blog, I will share two examples demonstrating how to provide seamless navigation in SAP Analytics Cloud.

These examples show how to enable end users to navigate between dashboards or transition from a dashboard to an application, including the use of filters and parameters.

Example 1: Generic hyperlinks in SAP Analytics Cloud (e.g. using a generic hyperlink to SAP S/4HANA to provide more context to your end users)

When creating a story in SAP Analytics Cloud, you may want to hyperlink to another story, a specific page in your story, or to a website. That was always possible and there is no scripting or something else. Anything can be maintained directly in SAC.


This can be a great way to provide more context to the message you’re trying to convey. 

So far so good but did you know that you can "apply selected dimension as filter" when connecting to a page within your story or a different story within your SAP Analytics Cloud environment?

Ok this is fine. Now let's go a step further. You can also create hyperlink to an external URL, which can be any jump target - e.g. a transaction in SAP S/4HANA or a Business Applications in SAP S/4HANA. And the interesting point is, that you can even pass any value provided within your SAC model in the hyperlink URL to parametrize your target application. 

And then you have all dimension values available to create a fully dynamic and generic URL to external applications/pages and can send the dimension values as parameters.

Include any dimension into your dynamic URL. You can insert it everywhere your parameter is needed.

You can even select if the ID or the description should be used.

But as we are talking about a fully dynamic hyperlinking, you can add the dimension ID or description also to the label of your generated hyperlink.

Which will lead to the following label.


And now let's see this in action. We will jump from SAP Analytics Cloud to SAP S/4HANA. We want to jump with the selected sales order number in SAP Analytics Cloud into a transaction in SAP S/4HANA. Let's see how this would look like.

Example number 2: Report-Report Interface Support in SAP Analytics Cloud

This is one of the SAP Analytics Cloud features I really like and which shows the deep live Integration between SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP BW.  We enhanced the data navigation in SAC by integrating the support of the Report-Report Interface (RRI), a feature traditionally available in SAP BW systems. RRI enables users to seamlessly transition between related reports, facilitating deeper data exploration and analysis. So in comparison to the hyperlinking in example 1, here anything needs to be maintained in the SAP BW backend. SAC will just leverage the settings plug & play.

Integration of RRI in SAP Analytics Cloud:

  • Functionality: RRI allows users to define "jump targets," enabling navigation from a source report to a target report or external web address. This is particularly useful for drilling down from summary reports to detailed analyses.

  • Implementation: To utilize RRI within SAC, configurations are made in the SAP BW backend using transaction RSBBS. Here, sender and receiver assignments are established, defining the source and target reports.

  • Usage in SAC: Once configured, users can access jump targets directly within SAC's Data Analyzer or stories. By right-clicking on a data point, the "Jump To" option appears, listing available targets for seamless navigation.

Benefits of RRI in SAC:

  • Enhanced Data Exploration: Users can move between reports without losing context, enabling comprehensive analysis across different data perspectives.

  • Improved Efficiency: RRI reduces the need to manually locate related reports, streamlining workflows and saving time.

  • Consistent User Experience: Integrating RRI into SAC provides a unified platform for data analysis, maintaining consistency across SAP tools.

I will show an easy example (though there are many many possibilities). In my case I have created a jump target in Report-Report Interface on the SAP BW backend side. 

This screenshot shows that there is an entry for my SAP BW query. All tools which uses this SAP BW Query (and support the Report-Report Interface) will make use of the entry (e.g. SAP Analytics Cloud, Analysis Office). 

You can make really specific assignments. Either you leave it fully generic, or you can define anything you want by using some specific options. In the generic mode the Report-Report Interface will try to identify and pass the filters to the right dimensions (if technical names are used for the SAP BW InfoObjects of course).

Anyhow my example will be easy. I will jump from a "high-level" SAP BW Query showing the cumulated sales values to a different SAP BW Query which shows "detailed" sales values for the specifc days. The Report-Report Interface notice that both queries use the same SAP BW InfoObjects (e.g. customer, order number with the relevant ID's) and passes the values from one query to another. As we are fully generic, we can jump from any dimension to the jump target.

In easy words: The job of the Report-Report Interface is nothing more that taking the dimension values (as filters) from a "sender" SAP BW Query and passes it to a "jump target" SAP BW Query. But wait, how this works? We use live models in SAC? That's the easy part. This works plug and play. All SAC live models which uses a specific SAP BW Query with specific Report-Report Interface assignments, will use this automatically. There is nothing to do on your side. The jump targets are visible in the context menu of SAP Analytics Cloud. 
If you don't make any specific settings about the jump target, the Data Analyzer in SAP Analytics Cloud will open. Let's watch an example.



Let's summarize thes both options

In a nutshell:

  • You can jump from any SAC widget to a page within the current story.
  • You can jump from any SAC widget to a different SAC story.
  • You can create dynamic URLs to external applications (both SAP and non-SAP).
  • In all cases, you can parameterize the target of the jump (in other words, you can pass filters).
  • This is a native built-in feature of SAP Analytics Cloud.
  • No scripting is needed.
  • It is fully dynamic and enhances user satisfaction.
  • Imagine analyzing information from SAP S/4HANA (e.g., orders, invoices, cost centers) in SAP Analytics Cloud and being able to jump directly from your analysis into the backend application. This is just one of many possibilities. You can even feed or trigger external APIs.
  • If you have SAP BW, you can leverage the deep integration between SAC and SAP BW and can make use pof the Report-Report-Interface. Either you jump to any SAP BW Query from any SAC Story and it will be called via the SAC Data Analyzer, or several other options.

These were just two examples and with the example 1 you can realize much more use cases. 
As long as the target application accepts parameters which can be passed by an URL.

And for those, who want to explore the other way round (from an external application to SAP Analytics Cloud), the SAP Analytics Cloud URL API is the way to go (but that is a different story): https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ANALYTICS_CLOUD/a4406994704e4af5a8559a640b496468/f0827b68d24548448060c526a8764d78.html?locale=en-US

Best regards

Adem

entry.title: Re: Code List based on CDS View doesn't show View Entities but only shows Custom Entities
entry.content:
Hello everyone!I'm more confused then Navi.
I don't know the RAP so let's take a step back.
On the S4 Hana on-premise system, I need to add a new custom field to an interface, let's say an order. The new field should refer to a custom table that will contain only code + description and will be updateable by the user via SM30.
Step 1 - Creation of new custom field.
This operation is possible using Fiori Extensibility -> Custom Field.
Step 2 - Associating the new field to the custom table + search help
I thought I could simply create a CDS View and associate that to the custom field created during the definition using "Code List based on CDS View".But apparently, this is not the case because or I did not understand what type of CDS View should be created since in the list I only find standard CDS Views, or I have no idea what's the way to do that.



Thanks a lot!

Dear Community | Estimada comunidad | Prezada comunidade,

SAP Signavio team together with SAP Signavio | Business Process Transformation Value Map built the “SAP Signavio Adoption Starter” series of Product webinars to help your with onboarding and adoption of your SAP Signavio solutions. 

 

The new schedule for these webinars for the first half of 2025 is now available for registration. You can find the dates through the registration links below. We continue to offer webinars in three languages: English, Portuguese, and Spanish.

Main changes:

  • The "SAP Signavio Adoption Starter: Process Explorer and Process Navigator" webinar has been renamed to "SAP Signavio Adoption Starter: Value Accelerators, Process Explorer and Process Navigator" to reflect the updated content focused on the adoption of SAP Signavio Value Accelerators. This webinar has been condensed into a one-day session.
  • The English webinars for APJ/EMEA will start at 10 am CET / 5 pm SGT.

entry.title: Re: Building a RAP Application: From Custom Actions to Sending Emails – A Step-by-Step Guide
entry.content:
Thanks for sharing! It's a valid demonstration of implementing actions in RAP model but I'm curious if there would actually be a valid use case for this specific scenario in real life. "Send me a random list of stuff" is not a very common requirement. Normally users want things in Excel, which is base functionality in Fiori, where such implementation would make sense.

Or I could also see a use case (still unlikely though) where the users get a list, then customize the layout and then send it by email. But this design wouldn't work for it, it seems. That's why I'd steer towards using separate functionality for the list/data and separate for email. Data is specific, email is generic.

This would also allow you to just use standard API for billing or sales documents. (It says "invoices" but that would be VBRK, VBAK is for sales documents. Either way, API exists for both.) And there might even be something for the emails.

I feel approach taken in this example (combining technically unrelated functionality, one of which is not specific to a business object) unfortunately leads us to the same road we've already been on with 1000-line reports, jumbo function modules, etc. The big ball of mud monolith that is difficult to maintain or even understand. It would be great to avoid past design mistakes in modern development.

Cheers!
